Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
267
1 INTRODUCTION
Vegetables are the fresh edible parts of some 
herbaceous plants such as roots, stems, leaves, 
flowers, fruit, or seeds. These plant components 
are consumed raw or prepared in a variety of 
ways, most often as a savory rather than sweet 
meal. For millennia, the term "vegetable" 
referred to any edible plant materials, including 
flowers, fruits, stalks, leaves, roots, and seeds. Another rather arbitrary concept is utilised in 
culinary and cultural traditions. Savoury fruits 
and vegetables like tomatoes and courgettes 
are included, as are blossoms like broccoli and 
seeds like pulses (Harri & Bianchini, Franca, 
2003; Melissa Petruzzello, 2018). Vegetables are produced throughout the 
whole world, with various degrees of success 
depending on the global climate. As a result of 
global agricultural trade, it is now feasible to 
buy vegetables grown hundreds of kilometers 
abroad, from subsistence farmers supporting 
their families' food needs to agribusinesses 
with huge acreages of single-product crops. After harvesting a crop, common procedures 
include grading, storage, processing, and 
selling.

Because they are abundant in vitamins, 
minerals, and dietary fiber while being low in 
fat and carbs, raw vegetables are an essential 
element of human nutrition. Many nutritionists 
recommend that people should consume five 
or more servings of fruits and vegetables each 
day. South Africa is one of the nations most likely 
to suffer severe water scarcity due to a lack of 
effective water management systems (Mjoli, 
2010). This will almost probably lead to water 
tensions between residential, agricultural, and 
industrial needs (Mjoli, 2010). Improvements 
in water use efficiency (WUE) in agriculture are 
necessary due to a lack of freshwater, growing 
input prices, and population growth. Increased 
water-use restrictions and severe droughts 
continue to have a negative impact on rural 
people in South Africa. South Africa is an arid country that ranks in the 
world's top 30 driest. South Africans use 237 
litres of water each day, which is more than the 
world average of 173 litres. Food and nutrition security is a major concern 
in South Africa.

Moreover, extreme weather 
occurrences such as El Nino and El Nina, as well 
as increasing temperatures, poor soil fertility, 
and unpredictable rainfall, will worsen food 
and nutrition security, negatively impacting 
resource-poor households. Another issue that contributes to the need 
is water quality . Water quality is the ability 
of a body of water to be safe, drinkable, and 
appealing to all forms of life on the planet. The already scarce fresh water in South Africa 
is deteriorating in quality due to increased 
pollution and the devastation of river catchments 
caused by urbanization, deforestation, river 
damming, wetlands destruction, industry, 
mining, agriculture, energy consumption, and 
accidental water contamination. Pollution and 
watershed degradation are becoming more 
prevalent as the human population rises. The amount of water on the earth is constant 
and cannot be raised or decreased, although it 
is distributed unevenly. South Africa receives only 300-400 mm of rain 
each year . This is nearly half the global average. As a result, South Africa is classified as a water-
stressed country.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
268
This manual does not provide a step-by-step 
action plan. There is no one-size-fits-all solution 
that can be applied to every scenario. Mulching, 
intercropping, conservation agriculture, 
crop rotation, integrated crop-livestock 
management, agroforestry, improved grazing, 
and better water management will all play a 
role in the transition to climate-smart vegetable 
production systems, as will innovative practices 
like better weather forecasting, more resilient 
food crops, and risk insurance. These strategies 
will need to adapt to local, regional, and global 
situations, and they may differ significantly from 
farmer to farmer .
As a result, this manual will provide solutions 
that can be tailored to various crop systems. 
It approaches the subject from a technical 
standpoint, such as research outputs. Definitions 
for technical terms are provided in some cases.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
269
2 CROP SELECTION
Figure 1 Grouping vegetables according to their water needs.
Source: Linda, 2019.
Crop selection is very important to crop farming 
success. It is a criterion that must be satisfied 
before starting a farming business. Even in 
the absence of a predetermined location and 
site for a farm, the crop to be grown can be 
chosen primarily based on its marketability and 
profitability, as well as the current farm conditions 
(biotic factors refer to living organisms and 
topographic features, various climatic factors 
such as water availability, temperature, and so 
on). Before planting, farmers should consider 
drought-tolerant vegetables that have been 
specifically chosen for drought tolerance, as well 
as those that thrive in hot, dry environments.

Among the most popular options are:
• Cowpeas, black-eyed peas and field peas
• Edible amaranth
• Quinoa
• Mustard greens
• Okra
• Summer squashes
• Most herbs
Drought-tolerant crops produce deeper root 
systems to adapt to both heat and low water 
levels. Many of them grow fast, reducing 
their long-term water requirements. Drought-
tolerant strains exist for several vegetables 
that demand a lot of water, such as the Black 
Diamond watermelon and the Heatwave II 
tomatoes. When looking for drought-resistant 
seeds and plants for gardening, look for labels 
that state the plant can thrive in higher temps 
and needs little to moderate water (Figure 1).

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
270
If you anticipate a dry year, instead of sowing 
seeds, buy seedlings to develop your garden. A nearby nursery will have a big selection of 
drought-tolerant vegetable seedlings. If you select a fast-maturing item, such as 
zucchini (which grows in approximately 60 days 
from seed to fruit), you will have a bountiful 
harvest within a month of putting it into your 
garden. If you must start from seeds, choose 
vegetables that develop quickly from seed to 
harvest, such as spring radishes, which may be 
harvested in less than 30 days. Different plants require different amounts of 
water for optimal growth. If you don't design 
your garden with the water demands of 
particular plant species in mind, you may end 
up over- and under-irrigating. Instead, focus on 
giving the right quantity of moisture to plants 
while conserving water by grouping vegetables 
in your garden based on their drought tolerance 
and watering requirements. 2.1 CLIMATE
The climate of a given region is defined as the 
temperature, humidity, daylight, and wind 
conditions of that location. Climate has a 
substantial impact on all stages and processes 
of vegetable development.

Water is an 
important consideration, and South Africa has 
been classified as one of the world's 30
th driest 
countries (Figure 2 ), with an annual average 
rainfall of less than 500 mm, significantly less 
than the global annual average rainfall of 860 
mm (FAO , 2016), and which is expected to 
decrease as a result of climate change due to 
global warming. Rainfall in South Africa is also unevenly 
distributed. The eastern half of the country is 
significantly wetter than the western half due to 
the nature of the weather . Droughts and floods 
occur regularly in South Africa, impacting the 
quantity of water available across the country. Figure 2 Annual rainfall distribution in South Africa. Source: Rand Water, 2021.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production

(%)
Agricultural use (including irrigation) 60
Environmental use 18
Urban and domestic use 11.5
Mining and industrial use 10.5
Growing season is defined as the period during 
which the conditions necessary for plants to 
mature and produce are present in your area 
for them to grow and produce. The length of the 
growing season is determined by the number of 
days that elapse between the last frost in the 
spring and the first frost in the fall, which is 
measured in days. The beginning and endpoints 
of the period of time during which plants 
develop from seed to maturity are represented 
by these two dates on a broad scale. Depending 
on where you live, it is possible that you will 
never see frost; instead, your dry season will 
serve as "winter ." You cannot control the length 
of your growing season because it is entirely 
determined by the climate in your region (Table 
2). Vegetable farmers should consider the 
following climatic parameters when growing 
vegetables. Table 2 Climatic conditions to be considered in the production area.

Temperature
• Hottest month: Maximum & minimum temperature
• Coldest month: Maximum & minimum temperature
Rainfall
• Average annual rainfall
• When the growing season is
• When the dry or wet season is
Position 
• Latitude and longitude
• Height above sea level
• Slope of the land
Soil 
• Soil type (sandy, silty, clay, mixture)
• pH
• Soil Salinity 
• Soil depth

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
272
• Temperature
What are the lowest and highest temperatures 
in the area? The temperature of an area 
determines which crops may be grown and 
when they can be planted. • Relative humidity
This can cause problems with irrigation 
scheduling. At sea level, the relative humidity 
is greater than on the Highveld. Infestation of 
diseases, such as mildew on pumpkins, can be 
worsened by hot and humid conditions. • Frost
The latest date of frost determines when frost-
sensitive crops may be sown. Frost may have 
an influence on the growing season of a crop. Planting a certain crop poses a risk if it takes 
a long growing season to develop and there is 
frost in the area. • Wind
The wind direction influences planting 
arrangement since it affects irrigation and crop 
development. Windbreaks may be necessary 
for high-wind areas. The continuous breeze 
affects water evaporation. • Rainfall
To plan your irrigation, you'll need to know 
how much rain has fallen. It is critical to deduct 
the quantity of water that must be applied at 
a specific period (monthly and/or seasonally) 
from the overall amount of water that must be 
applied. Watering must be timed according to 
the plant's developmental cycle.

• Hail
The history of hail falls should be kept since it 
is essential to plan around the risk of hail in a 
given location, as it can be a substantial risk that 
can result in crop loss, either partial or whole. • Radiation
The plot should receive at least six hours of 
direct sunlight per day. In the early stages of 
growth, however, some shadowing from natural 
or manufactured sources may be beneficial to 
keep young plants from wilting due to excessive 
sunshine. 2.2 QUALITY OF THE SOIL
Soil quality refers to a soil's ability to perform 
specific functions for specific land uses or within 
specific ecological boundaries. Despite the fact 
that this capacity is an inherent characteristic 
of soils, it varies from one to the next. Organic-
matter content, salinity, tilth, compaction, 
accessible nutrients, and rooting depth are 
just a few of the indicators that may be used to 
measure the health or condition of the soil in 
any given area, as well as its quality. • Slope
The slope of a field must be addressed when 
preparing and farming the soil. Erosion 
may easily occur if the slope is steep and 
inappropriate farming methods are utilised. • Soil conditions
The site should have enough drainage.

The most 
essential component of a farm is the soil, and it 
must be well-managed. The percentage clay, pH, 
kind of soil, soil texture, and planting depth are 
all important factors to consider . Each location 
has its own type of soil, and understanding the 
soil's history and keeping track of agricultural 
operations is essential for crop rotation. Quality loam to clay loam soils are suitable for 
vegetable cultivation as depicted in Table 3.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
273
Sandy soils may be troublesome due to excessive 
water and nutrient drainage, as well as the 
presence of nematodes. All of these variables 
will result in crop stress and lower yields. Good soil is required for a high yield. All of 
the nutrients necessary for healthy plant 
growth must be present in the soil, as well as 
a structure that permits plants to stand erect. The soil structure must be capable of retaining 
sufficient air and water for plant roots while 
allowing excess water to drain. The bulk of 
nutrients are recycled from the soil naturally 
by plant roots and returned to the soil by 
falling leaves and other organic debris. Organic 
waste is consumed by earthworms and other 
creatures and converted to humus, which 
darkens and strengthens the topsoil. Humus is 
readily removed or washed away if the soil is 
left exposed. In most instances, the subsurface 
yields less. The greatest physical quality of the soil is 
contributed by fresh organic matter that 
decomposes fast. The presence of moisture, 
nitrogen, and warm temperatures accelerates 
decomposition. Organic matter slows the loss 
of minerals through soil leaching until they are 
liberated by microorganisms.

Organic waste 
breakdown increases soil structure, allowing 
roots to penetrate deeper into the soil. Organic 
matter incorporation can enhance the water-
holding capacity of sands and sandy soils, 
whereas aggregation in heavy soils can aid in 
drainage. Crop rotation is required to maintain 
high soil productivity. Although some soil is 
naturally fertile, the majority of soil is infertile 
or has lost nutrients as a result of bad farming 
methods. Table 3 Depicts some of the characteristics of typical soil types. Soil type Features Methods of improvement
Sand Less than 10% clay, won’t aggregate or 
will slightly aggregate
Regularly add organic matter and 
fertiliser, use green manure crops
Sandy loam 15-20% clay, forms a stable ball without 
difficulty
Add organic matter, fertiliser and use 
green manure
Sandy clay loam 
to clay loam
20-40% clay, can be rolled into a stable 
sausage
Organic matter is less important. The 
amount of fertiliser required will be 
determined by soil analysis. Clay More than 40% clay, good water holding 
capacity, very hard when it dries
Add organic materials, compost, and 
gypsum
Silt Poor structure, good fertility Add loose organic matter, use green 
manure

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
274
A soil laboratory should examine the nutritional 
quality of the soil on a regular basis. The optimum 
rate of fertiliser application is determined by the 
nutritional state of the soil. It is usually a good 
idea to have your soil tested. Enquire with your 
local extension service or a fertiliser company 
for assistance.

The soil analysis is broken down into two sections: 
soil characterization and fertility status, and soil 
fertilisation recommendations. The study covers 
a variety of topics, including soil characteristics 
(pH, texture, percent exchangeable sodium, 
percent organic matter, and salinity expressed 
as electrical conductivity ). The plant's fertility 
condition is described in terms of nutrients 
available to it. The second section, named 
"fertility recommendation," lists the amounts 
of fertiliser that should be used. These amounts 
are determined by the crop requirements, crop 
management strategies that affect the crop (as 
described in the information sheet), existing 
soil fertility levels, and the producer's desired 
yield target. A special notification is given if the 
results of the tests suggest that a salt or sodium 
hazard exists, or if the information provided 
reveals any other unique problems. The pH of the soil, for example, determines 
whether it is acidic or alkaline in nature. The pH 
of acidic soil is 7, while the pH of alkaline soil is 
7. A pH of less than 7 indicates that the soil is 
acidic, whereas a pH of greater than 7 indicates 
that the soil is neutral.

A plant that grows well 
in acidic soil will suffer in alkaline soil, while a 
plant that grows well in alkaline soil will struggle 
in acidic soil. Without a soil testing kit, it's 
impossible to detect the pH content of the soil 
but local farmers and extension officials should 
be able to tell you whether it's neutral, acidic, 
or alkaline. 2.3 WATER
Water is vital in the process of transporting 
nutrients and carbohydrates from the soil to 
the plants. At some point in their existence, 
all plants will be harmed by water scarcity or 
drought. This can be lethal to plants or lead 
them to grow at a much slower rate in some 
situations. Plants that have been exposed to 
extended drought may be more susceptible to 
secondary infection by pests and diseases than 
they would be otherwise. The following are 
some examples of water parameters that can 
affect plant growth:
2.3.1 Water availability
The amount of water required to grow 
vegetables at maximum yield might be rather 
large. On a 1 000 m
2 (very small scale) of 
vegetable growing, 15000-20000 litres of water 
are required per week. Vegetable production will be limited to 100-200 
m
2 if a borehole is the sole accessible water 
source and does not supply water at a rate of at 
least a litre/sec.

If municipal water is used, the cost should be 
carefully calculated. Select irrigation systems that are appropriate 
for the land and crops being grown. The system 
must be easy to use and maintain. 2.3.2 Water quality
Another significant factor to consider is the 
water quality. If the water quality is inadequate, 
it will have a negative impact on the soil quality, 
which will have a negative impact on the crop 
quality. Wastewater (greywater) is, for example,

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
275
the water that comes from sinks, bathtubs, 
showers, and washing machines, among other 
places. Water for the garden usually does not 
need to be treated with chemicals or biological 
agents before use. It is vital to consider the 
following while using wastewater for your 
garden: wastewater that contains an excessive 
amount of dirt should be removed. The water 
from a washing machine's final rinse, on 
the other hand, can only be used in modest 
amounts. The safest sort of wastewater is bath 
or shower water . There is a scarcity of clear 
scientific information on the long-term impacts 
of bath/shower water on soils after extensive 
use. When this product has been used in the 
past, no detrimental impacts on the soil or plants 
have been observed. According to estimations, 
excellent soil with a high organic matter content 
can hold about 25 L of wastewater per square 
metre of the surface area every week. Using 
wastewater in a cycle with freshwater to purify 
the soil of possibly dangerous chemicals is 
an effective method. To aid in the natural 
decomposition of waste leftovers, thick mulches 
should be spread to areas that are maintained 
by wastewater .

2.3.3 Watering methods
For all vegetable crops, good yields are 
impossible without enough water during the 
entire growing season. Some crops require more 
water than others, and the water requirements 
of various cultivars of the same crop might 
vary. The following are merely suggestions that 
should be tweaked depending on the season 
and conditions on your plot or garden:
Seedlings
The critical time is between sowing and the 
emergence of the seedlings. At all times the 
soil in contact with the seed must be moist. Transplants
Watering is necessary before and after 
transplanting, especially in hot weather when 
immature plants can't replace water lost via 
their leaves. Leafy crops
Leafy crops (such as cabbage and spinach) 
require roughly 25 litres per square meter 
per week of water and should be actively 
developing from the time they are seeded 
or transplanted. Any interruption in the 
water supply might have an impact on yield 
and quality. In the summer, splitting the 
water supply into two applications may be 
preferable. As the crops mature, more water 
should be applied. In cooler months (winter), 
however, single weekly irrigation is sufficient.

Root crops 
The typical weekly water requirement for 
potatoes, carrots, and sweet potatoes is 
between 10 and 15 litres per square meter . In the absence of rain, regular watering is 
required, with the goal of ensuring that water 
permeates deeply into the soil. Poor root 
development is hampered by insufficient 
irrigation. When tubers begin to form, 
potatoes require more water . Other crops
The average water need for crops including 
green beans, green peas, pumpkin, tomato, 
and cowpea is 25 litres per square meter, 
but this varies depending on the stage 
of development. Excessive leaf growth is 
encouraged by too much water up to this 
point. Water-efficient vegetables
Cowpeas, amaranth, pigeon peas, and 
Bambara, for example, are drought-tolerant 
crops that may be cultivated in rain-fed 
environments if the rain is evenly distributed.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
276
2.4 CROPS AND CULTIVARS
When determining which crops to grow in 
a given year, it is vital to make an informed 
decision. The first condition is that your crop has 
a market that is appropriate for it. Determine 
the market's size, the distance between the 
market and its location, as well as the quality 
and quantity of products that the market 
requires. Make sure you understand the crop's 
climatic requirements and compare them to the 
climate in your area to see if the crop will grow 
in your area of production. Crop selection is also influenced by the types 
of nutrients that the crops under consideration 
may provide. Vitamin A is one of the nutrients 
in our diets that is badly lacking, so dark-green 
leafy vegetables and orange vegetables should 
be ingested on a regular basis. Carrots, butternut 
squashes, and orange-fleshed sweet potatoes, 
as well as leafy vegetables like Amaranthus and 
spinach, are among the most significant crops 
to plant in your vegetable garden. It's crucial to choose the right cultivars. A 
tomato, for example, is more than just a tomato; 
there are numerous varieties, including short 
growers, long growers, processing cultivars, 
and fresh market cultivars.

To guarantee that 
the appropriate cultivar is selected for all 
vegetable crops, it's a good idea to first figure 
out who the crop's target market will be. The 
most successful technique is to talk to several 
seed firms and figure out which cultivars are 
best for production in your area and for selling 
in your market. The seed firm wants to know 
what the summer and winter temperatures will 
be when the first frost will occur, and how long 
each season will persist in each site. The most 
successful method is to talk to several seed 
firms and figure out which cultivars are best for 
production in your area and for selling in your 
market. The seed firm wants to know what the 
summer and winter temperatures will be when 
the first frost will occur, and how long each 
season will persist in each site. 2.5 SIZE, SUPPLIES AND COST

ANALYSIS
Producing vegetables is an expensive endeavor. High input and labor expenses are required 
to produce vegetables commercially, and as a 
result, the best possible yield and quality are 
required. Almost all of the aforementioned 
aspects have an impact on costs and financial 
resources. You should abandon the concept if 
you don't have enough money to purchase all of 
the necessary inputs for vegetable cultivation. The materials that will be required, such as 
fertiliser, seeds/seedlings, and chemicals, will 
be determined as part of the planning phase. The size of the garden or field will dictate the 
type of cultivation procedures and equipment 
that will be used. If the fields are small, the task 
can be accomplished by hand, requiring the use 
of tools such as garden forks, rakes, hand hoes, 
and a wheelbarrow. In all other circumstances, 
a tractor with the necessary implements is 
required. Because vegetable seeds are often 
very small, successful planting necessitates a 
fine seedbed. The size of the plots that will be created will be 
determined by the volume of products that will 
be sold. Plant in accordance with an anticipated 
marketing date as well.

When deciding on the arrangement of the 
vegetable plots, keep in mind the crop rotation 
strategy that will be utilised.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
277
2.6 BIOLOGY OF PLANT PESTS

AND DISEASES
Vegetable growers are constantly confronted 
with insect pests and diseases that wreak 
havoc on their crops (Figure 3 ). Typically, 
they'd seen these in past seasons, and the 
symptoms appear to be the same. There are 
times, however, when an atypical pest (not 
native to South Africa) may appear . Exotic 
pests are a source of concern for farmers, 
as they have the potential to disrupt the 
agricultural and horticulture industries, as 
well as raise production costs and consumer 
prices (Hoffmann, Learmonth & Wood, 2014).
Pest and disease control can be exceedingly 
costly, even prohibitively so in some cases. If a 
farmer chooses to apply pesticides, he or she is 
liable for the expense of the chemicals, spraying 
equipment, and protective clothes. Pesticides 
are sometimes applied haphazardly, causing 
environmental damage. Disease control may 
need extra effort on the farmer's or gardener's 
part. It's possible that this is one of his or her 
creations. He or she could also be forced to pay 
for the services of others in order to carry out 
disease control initiatives. Small-scale farmers 
who cannot afford these management methods 
may experience a different form of loss than 
their larger counterparts.

They may need to sell their product as quickly 
as possible due to its limited shelf life. For the 
development of a disease, the presence of 
the three components listed below (Scholthof, 
2006) (Figure 4) is required.
• Pathogen
• Host
• Favorable environmental conditions
2.6.1 Diseases
The plants that they infect and reproduce on 
provide the energy for vegetable diseases. 
They are typically encouraged by wet weather, 
poor drainage, and limited airflow, in addition 
to causing severe damage. Plant diseases can 
cause mouldy coatings, wilting, scabs, blotches, 
rusts, and decay, to name a few symptoms 
(Bost, 2021).
2.6.1.1 Disease transmission 
Windblown, rain, or dust are common ways for 
invasive species to travel from plant to plant 
in vegetable crops (Table 4 ). Humans spread 
germs through a variety of techniques, including 
plant cultivation, grafting, pruning, and moving 
infected plant material. Animals such as insects 
and mites, in addition to humans, are common 
disease vectors (lvarez-Hernández, 2019).

any other damaging factor. • Type of crop. • Age of host plant: Some hosts become more susceptible to a particular disease

• Why is accurate diagnosis important? Disease control requires a significant investment 
of time, effort, and money. A faulty diagnosis can 
result in control errors that are both damaging 
and expensive. As a precautionary measure, one 
should strive to become as proficient at making 
diagnoses based on symptoms as possible in 
case expert assistance is unavailable and the 
situation demands it. • Identification of pests and diseases
Due to a lack of knowledge, it might be difficult 
for a farmer or gardener to effectively diagnose 
pests and illnesses in the field. He or she will need 
to call a plant pathologist or an entomologist 
who works in a laboratory in these cases (for 
example, the Agricultural Research Council, 
Vegetable, Industrial and Medicinal Plants). ̶ INSECTS
It is vital to inspect the crop at least once a week 
to see if any pests are present. Chemicals are 
only used to counteract them if they are present 
and in harmful amounts. ̶ DISEASES
Because disease organisms are microscopic, 
they cannot be seen and therefore their 
arrival and accumulation in the crop cannot 
be monitored as easily as the arrival and 
accumulation of insect pests in the crop.

To 
protect the crop from possible infection, it is 
possible that regular preventative sprays will be 
required in some cases. ̶ SYMPTOMS
Since disease organisms are small, they can't 
be seen, thus their arrival and buildup in the 
crop can't be tracked as easily as insect pests. In 
rare circumstances, regular prophylactic sprays 
may be necessary to protect the crop against 
infection. In other circumstances, the farmer's 
observations and careful sampling can make 
the difference between a correct diagnosis and 
none at all. It is helpful for farmers to know what types of 
symptoms to look for, even if they do not do the 
final diagnosis. • Expand awareness of what is going on 
around
You must be aware of what is going on in 
your immediate surroundings. You must know 
whether summers are typically hot and dry or 
whether they are typically mild and wet; in other 
words, you must know the climate. You must 
know whether it was warm and rainy during the 
previous week or the previous month. You also need to know what kinds of crops 
people are growing in your area and the way in 
that they are commonly produced in the area.

Certain disease patterns in your area become 
more apparent if you pay attention to growers as 
they describe what they do and what problems 
they encounter .

2.6.2 Pests
The presence of insect pests in any vegetable 
garden can cause significant problems. Insects 
feeding on the plant may prevent the plant 
from growing properly, resulting in a poor crop 
or even a complete crop loss. The use of natural 
and biological methods to reduce the damage 
caused by insect pests is becoming increasingly 
popular in the long run.

There are cultivars of 
various vegetables, for example, that have been 
specifically bred to be pest-resistant (Masabni, 
Dainello & Cotner, 2009). Developing an integrated pest management 
(IPM) strategy that incorporates insect-resistant 
cultivars as well as environmentally friendly 
pest control methods can help to reduce insect 
damage. When growing vegetables, keep the 
following considerations in mind:
• In some form or another, pests attack 
all vegetable crops to varying degrees of 
severity. To effectively control an insect 
infestation, it is critical to first identify the 
type of insect that is attacking your crop as 
accurately as possible. • Keep track of pest and disease problems 
throughout the season so that you can 
plan and prepare better for the following 
season. The success of vegetable gardening 
is greatly enhanced by the implementation 
of a pest and disease management 
programme. • When it comes to reducing the need 
for chemical control, good agricultural 
practices and maintaining high fertility 
and vigorous growth are two of the most 
effective methods. • Pesticides should be used with caution.

The 
first rule of safety is to carefully read the 
instructions on the container's label and to 
follow them exactly as they are written
• Before purchasing a pesticide, check to 
see if it contains a list of the pests that will 
be controlled. Use the proper products 
in accordance with the manufacturer's 
instructions, and avoid using higher 
concentrations or larger dosages than what 
is recommended. • Store pesticides out of reach of children and 
animals, behind locked doors, and away 
from food. Always store pesticides in their 
original, clearly labelled containers, and 
properly dispose of any empty containers 
after they have been used. 2.6.3 Common vegetable pests 
Pests that attack leafy vegetables include flies, 
bugs, mites, worms, and molluscs, to name a 
few. Flies, bugs, mites, worms, and molluscs 
are the most common pests that attack leafy 
vegetables. Here is a list of the most common 
pests of leaf vegetables and information on how 
to prevent, control, and manage them if you 
grow greens or plan to grow them.

2.6.3.1 American bollworm
Beans, brassicas, peas, potatoes, and tomatoes 
are among the crops that are attacked by the 
bolworm, (shown in Figure 5 ). Black, brown, 
green, or pink larvae with an off-white stripe 
running along the sides and abdomen are 
common colours for this species. It can cause 
damage to the leaves, flowers, pods, and fruit 
of a plant.
2.6.3.2 Aphids
With soft, pear-shaped bodies that are 
predominantly green or black, these insects 
(see Figure 6 ) are typically found in colonies 
sucking sap from the new tender growth parts 
of most vegetables.

With an orange cross on its back and spots and

Usually grey, waxy worm-like grubs as in Figure

They can be found in the upper soil surface and

Brown flies with yellow bands or spots on their

Caterpillars are green when young, but older

A green caterpillar that feeds on the outside of

Minute/very small insects that can be found

It primarily targets beans, pumpkins,

range of other crops. 2.6.3.11 Red spider mites
Microscopic, spider-like insects that range in

2.7 WEEDS
Weed is a general term used to describe any 
plant that grows in an area where it is not 
wanted. Since the beginning of human attempts 
to cultivate plants, humans have had to contend 
with the invasion of weeds into areas designated 
for crop production. Some undesirable plants 
were later discovered to have virtues that were 
not initially suspected, and as a result, they 
were removed from the category of weeds and 
placed under cultivation. When other cultivated 
plants were transplanted to new climates, they 
escaped cultivation and became weeds or 
invasive species, resulting in the extinction of 
the cultivar . As a result, the category of weeds 
is constantly evolving, and the term itself is a 
relative one (Chandrasena, 2019). 
• Weeds compete with plants for resources 
such as light, nutrients, water, and soil.
• Can serve as a breeding ground for pests 
and diseases.
• Endanger the health of both animals and 
humans.
• It interferes with the operation of the farm.
Because of this, weeds must be eliminated. In 
a vegetable garden, weeds are typically pulled 
out by hand or with a hand hoe, which is a tool 
specifically designed for weeding.
2.8 CONVENTIONAL VEGETABLES

VERSUS INDIGENOUS OR

TRADITIONAL VEGETABLES
The dietary diversity, food security, and 
livelihoods of populations across Sub-
Saharan Africa are enhanced by the presence 
of approximately 1000 different species of 
indigenous and naturalised vegetables. These 
foods are also important components of 
alimentary traditions and cultural identity, but 
they have been neglected and underutilised 
as a result of their status as a threatened 
and endangered species. Slowly but steadily, 
universities, research centres, and development 
organisations are beginning to pay attention to 
African vegetables (Figure 13).

OF VEGETABLES TO DROUGHT

TOLERANCE
To combat both heat and low water levels, 
drought-tolerant vegetables develop deep root 
systems. Because many of them grow quickly, 
they have lower water requirements in the 
long run as well. Difficult-to-grow vegetables, 
such as the Black Diamond watermelon and 
the Heatwave II tomatoes, are now available 
in drought-tolerant varieties. In your search for 
seeds and plants suitable for drought-resistant 
gardening, look for labels that state the plant 
prefers hot temperatures and has low to 
moderate water requirements (Linda, 2019).
• Seed color as an important 
morphological characteristic
The morphological characteristics of landraces 
and indigenous crops are identified based on 
the hypothesis that they are associated with 
performance, such as the hypothesis that seed 
colour is associated with drought tolerance. A 
study confirmed that the colour of the seeds 
has an impact on early crop establishment. 
Compared to light coloured seeds, dark-coloured 
seeds had a better chance of emergence. 
Under field conditions, this trend was able to 
be extrapolated successfully (e.g. groundnuts, 
wild melon, maize landraces, cowpeas and wild 
mustard).

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production

• Cultivar selection 
Seed companies invest a great deal of time and 
money in research so that the best cultivar for 
each region can be identified and the necessary 
data can be collected to aid in the decision-
making process. It is also necessary to include 
information from local comparative strip trials 
that have been conducted by study groups and 
individual farmers.
The risky option is to rely solely on the advice 
of a neighbour because the current season 
will be different from the previous one. As a 
result of combining all of the information, the 
identity of three to five cultivars that can be 
used will be quickly determined. It is critical to 
choose cultivars with a short growing season, 
i.e., those that mature early, to achieve better 
performance under drought conditions.
• Market-driven horticultural characteristics
• Maturity required to coincide with cropping 
season, supply the market and reduce the 
risk of weather-related crop failure
• Resistance to diseases, insects, stress, 
and physiological disorders that can be 
depended on is essential (For example, 
blossom-end rot)
2.10 SEED PRODUCTION 
SYSTEMS – ACCESS TO

CLIMATE-RESILIENT PLANTING

MATERIAL THAT IS DROUGHT 
RESISTANT (FARMER 
PRODUCTION SYSTEMS)
Given the complexity that African agriculture is 
facing as a result of climate-induced stresses, 
increasing agricultural resilience should be a top 
priority. Seed systems are critical for enhancing 
such resilience because seed security is directly 
linked to food security and, in general, to the 
ability to sustain one's livelihood. Seed systems 
are critical in ensuring that farmers have access 
to adaptable crops and varieties, as well as the 
flexibility to obtain seed when they need it 
(Kansiime & Mastenbroekvan, 2016). Farmer 
seed systems, which include the following, 
provide this seed security:
• The right seeds
Smallholders can only gain access to crops that 
are not prioritised by the private sector, but 
which are locally adapted, drought-resistant, 
and important for household food and nutrition 
by way of seed production systems, which are 
primarily focused on smallholders. These are 
typically grown by women for women.

• The right time
Farmers can access what they need quickly 
if they have access to locally available farmer 
seed, which is critical in the current context 
of shortages in the formal seed sector and if 
farmers are forced to plant early or replant as a 
result of unpredictable weather . • The right price
Farmer seed systems offer low-cost seed to 
smallholder farmers who cannot afford to make 
annual purchases of hybrid maize seed, let alone 
the even more expensive package of chemical 
inputs required to unlock their yield potential. As the economy continues to deteriorate, they 
provide an important safety net for vulnerable 
communities, as well as a lifeline for smallholder 
farmers with limited financial and technical 
resources. Furthermore, farmer seed systems 
provide reservoirs of plant genetic resources 
that farmers will need to continue to adapt to 
climate change in the future. Farmers surveyed 
by the farmer field schools look for certain traits 
that they need to adapt to local conditions, 
primarily high-yielding, drought-tolerant, and 
early maturing varieties.

3 SOIL MANAGEMENT
3.1 ORGANIC MATTER OF SOILS
Soil organic matter (SOM) is the organic matter 
component of soil, consisting of plant and animal 
detritus at various stages of decomposition, cells 
and tissues of soil microbes, and substances 
synthesised by soil microbes. SOM has a variety 
of beneficial effects on the physical and chemical 
properties of soil, as well as on the soil's ability 
to provide regulatory ecosystem services. SOM 
is particularly important for the functions and 
quality of the soil. Soil organic matter (SOM) benefits soil function 
through a variety of complex, interactive 
edaphic factors. A partial list of these benefits 
to soil function includes improvements in soil 
structure and aggregation, water retention and 
biodiversity, pollutant absorption and retention, 
buffering capacity, as well as the cycling and 
storage of plant nutrients. SOM increases soil 
fertility by providing cation exchange sites 
and acting as a reservoir of plant nutrients, 
particularly nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and 
sulphur (S), as well as micronutrients, which are 
slowly released as a result of the mineralization 
of SOM over time.

As a result, the amount of 
SOM present in the soil and soil fertility are 
highly correlated. SOM also serves as a major sink and source of 
soil carbon, according to the USDA (C). In spite 
of the fact that the carbon (C) content of soil 
organic matter (SOM) varies considerably, SOM 
is typically estimated to contain 58 percent 
C, and the term "soil organic carbon" (SOC) is 
frequently used as a synonym for SOM, with 
measured soil organic carbon content often 
serving as a proxy for SOM. Solvents in soil 
are one of the world's largest carbon sinks, 
and their contribution to the global carbon 
cycle is significant. As a result, the dynamics 
of SOM/SOC and the ability of soils to provide 
the ecosystem service of carbon sequestration 
through SOM management have recently 
received a great deal of attention. When it comes to most upland soils, the 
concentration of SOM in the soil generally 
ranges from 1 percent to 6 percent of the 
total mass of topsoil. Soils with less than 1% 
organic matter in their upper horizons are most 
common in deserts, whereas soils in low-lying, 
wet areas can have up to 90% SOM in their 
upper horizons. Organic soils are those that 
have 12 to 18 percent soil organic carbon (SOC) 
in their composition.

The living biomass of microbes can be divided 
into three categories: fresh and partially 
decomposed detritus, and humus (composted 
organic matter). Surface plant litter, also known 
as fresh vegetal detritus, is typically excluded 
from the composition of SOM.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
291
3.2 HOW TO MAKE COMPOST
• Where to make a compost heap? Build the compost pile on soil or grass rather 
than concrete or asphalt to take advantage of 
the earthworms and soil microorganisms that 
will migrate from the soil into the compost pile 
as they break down organic matter . Building 
a pile in a warm, partly shady spot that is 
protected from too much wind and sunlight 
will prevent the pile from drying out too quickly 
in warm, dry climates (it must have at least 6 
hours of full sunlight per day). Build the pile in 
a sunny location if you live in a cool climate. Do 
not place the pile in an area where water may 
collect. Excessive moisture at the bottom of the 
pile may halt the composting process or cause 
odor issues. • What can be used in making compost? Composting is a viable option for approximately 
half of the waste we generate each day. Almost 
anything that was once alive or was once a part 
of something alive can be used in a compost 
pile, and it does not have to be organic. Look at 
the activators in Table 5 for more information. • Building a compost heap 
 » A good size for the compost heap is 2 
spades by 2 spades (2m x 2m).

» The heap should be about twice as big 
at the bottom as at the top. » Start by levelling the spot for compost 
making. » Have all the material to be used for the 
compost ready. Table 5 Compost making. Activators Other materials you can add Very slow to rot

bags

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
292
All large pieces should be cut into smaller 
pieces before being assembled. Do not use 
more than one type of material in a layer . It 
is critical to maintaining the proper balance 
between carbon-rich (brown) and nitrogen-
rich (green) materials. The rate of composting 
and the quality of the finished compost can be 
affected by the way certain types of material 
are mixed or the proportions of the materials 
are changed. Ideally, 3 parts brown to 2 parts 
green and 1 part manure should be used in this 
ratio. A large number of browns will decompose 
too slowly, whereas a large number of greens 
will produce an odour .

Assemble enough of the 
following materials to form a pile that is at least 
2 metres long, 2 metres wide, and 1 metre high: 
Carbon-rich brown (dry) material:
• Wood shavings, sawdust, hay, wheat straw 
and corn stalks, shredded cardboard and 
newspaper
• Green (fresh) material: Nitrogen-rich 
materials such as grass clippings, fresh 
leaves and twigs from harvested plants, 
thinned seedlings, fruit and vegetable 
waste (such as peelings), tea bags, and egg 
shells can all be used
• Animal manure that is high in nitrogen: 
fresh chicken, cattle, or horse manure are 
examples
Compost piles develop most effectively when 
they are constructed in layers. A good way to 
ensure that the material is added in the proper 
proportion is to layer it on top of each other as 
follows: 
Base layer
• Loosen the soil in the area where you will 
be constructing the compost heap. This 
will aid in the growth of bacteria and fungi. Microorganisms in the heap require air to 
break down and decompose the organic 
material they are consuming
• Place a 15-20 cm layer of dry brown material 
on the ground to begin the pile building 
process 
• The base must be constructed in such a 
way that sufficient air can pass through 
it.

If possible, it is preferable to add water 
to each layer of dry material rather than 
attempting to wet the entire pile after it has 
been constructed. • The entire pile should be as wet as a sponge 
that has been thoroughly wrung. After the 
dry material layer has been constructed, it 
should be moistened but not soaked. First layer
• To establish the first layer, spread a 10-15 
cm layer of fresh green material on the 
ground. A pitchfork can be used to quickly 
mix the layers together for better results. Second layer
• Use kraal or chicken manure, mature 
compost, or bone meal to cover the top 
layer of the first layer with a layer about 
10 cm deep. The manure, old compost, 
bone-meal, and blood-meal speed up the 
decomposition of the compost heap. Third layer
• Lastly, apply a thin layer of soil and ash 
approximately 3 cm deep
• You should repeat the process (base layer, 
first layer, second layer, third layer) three or 
four more times or until you have used up 
all of the material
• As you build the compost heap, moisten 
each layer with water as you go
• The organisms in the soil, in particular 
earthworms, will aid in the rotting process 
by mixing the material and increasing 
airflow

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
293
• A top layer of soil, dry grass, leaves, or 
sawdust is required to keep odours in 
and to deter flies from entering. When 
the construction of the compost heap is 
completed, take two sticks of about 2m in 
length and push them into the heap, shake 
these from side to side and pull them out, 
so that the pile can be aerated
Cover
• Straw, soil, old sacks, or plastic can be used 
to cover the heap. Moisture and heat are 
kept in by this method
Heat
• Contribute to the decomposition of plant 
and animal matter as well as the destruction 
of weeds and weed seeds
• After approximately three weeks, the 
compost must be turned over, covered, 
and allowed to re-heat to its original 
temperature. When the compost is free of 
offensive odours and is a dark brown color, 
it is ready to be utilised
When it comes to vegetables, compost should 
not be used on the following: 
• Carrots and beetroot because it has a 
tendency to cause split and malformed 
roots
• Beans because it attracts harmful insects
• Onions, because it causes early bolting and 
thick-necked onions, which are undesirable 
characteristics

3.3 MULCHES (DROUGHT-
RESISTANT SOIL)
Garden soil can behave in two ways: as a sieve, 
allowing water to filter down quickly, or as a 
sponge, allowing water to soak in slowly. Soil 
that is porous and drought-resistant holds water, 
allowing the plant to have a constant supply 
of moisture. To improve drought resistance, 
incorporate high-quality organic material (such 
as compost) into drought-resistant soil at the 
start of each growing season. If one wants to reduce evaporation or limit the 
amount of water that evaporates before it can 
be absorbed into the soil, one should plant 
more water-wise plants. The use of a thick 
layer of organic mulch in a garden or field saves 
water more quickly, easily, and economically 
than any other method available. Mulch aids in 
the conservation of moisture, the prevention 
of water runoff, and the improvement of soil 
health, all of which contribute to stronger, 
deeper root growth and improved plant health. Organic mulch should be applied around 
drought-tolerant vegetables at a depth of 
5-7cm. Straw mulch is an excellent choice for 
vegetable gardens. It decomposes over time, 
releasing organic material and nutrients into 
the soil as a result.

Mulches are divided into 
two categories: organic mulches and inorganic 
mulches. Molasses derived from animal or 
plant materials are known as organic mulches; 
on the other hand, inorganic mulches include 
gravel, crushed rock, and synthetic plastic in a 
variety of colours. When compared to plastic 
mulch, organic mulches such as dry grass, 
sawdust, and paper would have the advantage 
of decomposing in the soil at the end of the 
growing season, gradually increasing soil organic 
matter and contributing to waste reduction. Aside from providing soil cover, mulching 
also serves several other purposes, including 
preventing soil water loss through evaporation, 
delaying weed growth and promoting the more 
efficient use of nutrients, maintaining good 
soil structure, and protecting harvested crops 
from contamination by soil. It also serves to 
protect the soil from erosion caused by water 
and wind, as well as hail damage. It is true that 
herbicide application is effective at suppressing 
the growth of weeds, but it can have negative 
consequences for soil ecosystems and the 
environment, such as decreasing soil microbial 
activity and increasing water contamination.

It is possible for smallholder and commercial 
farmers, depending on the type of material used, 
to increase yield while simultaneously lowering 
the costs associated with vegetable cultivation 
by mulching their fields. Mulch application has 
been shown to improve water use efficiency 
(WUE), crop growth, and yield, according to the 
research. In the root zone, mulch directs excess 
rainwater away from the plant's roots, allowing 
the moisture regime to remain more consistent. Mulches are defined as a thick layer (50-150 
mm) of organic material that is spread over bare 
soil to protect it from the elements. Mulches 
have a number of advantages that come with 
their use, including the following:
• Regulates soil moisture and temperature
• Enhances water infiltration
• Prevents soil erosion
• Suppresses weeds 
• Prevents crop damage
Wet, low-lying soil should not be mulched. Also 
keep in mind that mulch can attract termites and 
has other drawbacks, such as being less long-
lasting and potentially creating a fire hazard. Organic mulches can be made from a variety of 
sources, including the following:

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
295
Lawn mowing
It is high in nitrogen and can be used as a 
mulching material. The mowings from most 
lawns contain more than 1% nitrogen and 2% 
potash, respectively. The mowing of lawns can 
also be used as green manure, which can be 
worked into the soil and used to make compost.
Straw
This is clean, contains few weed seeds when 
purchased directly from farms, is inexpensive 
when purchased in bulk, and is quick and 
simple to lay down. Dig it into the soil during 
the autumn, and by the spring, it will have 
assimilated and become indistinguishable from 
the rest of the soil.

and is quick and 
simple to lay down. Dig it into the soil during 
the autumn, and by the spring, it will have 
assimilated and become indistinguishable from 
the rest of the soil. Wood shavings/sawdust
It is generally agreed that sawdust and wood 
shavings are safe and effective soil improvers 
that do not acidify the soils when used in 
appropriate quantities. Additionally, it aids in 
soil aeration while also increasing the soil's 
moisture-holding capacity. When sawdust is 
used, it should be thoroughly decomposed 
before being used. Newspaper
Unlike most other materials, newspapers have 
excellent moisture-retaining properties. A layer 
of hay, straw, or wood shavings placed over the 
paper will improve its appearance while also 
preventing the paper from being blown away 
by the wind. Paper is extremely effective at 
inhibiting the growth of weeds. Because it is 
dense enough to prevent sunlight from readily 
passing through to the soil, it is most effective 
when applied after irrigation or precipitation. Paper eventually decomposes and contributes 
humus to the soil, albeit at a slow rate. Plastic mulches
Plastic covers are inorganic and extremely 
effective, but they are only partially compatible 
with drip irrigation systems in most cases.

When 
planting early in the spring, plastic can also be 
used to raise the temperature of the soil. Figure 15 Examples of mulching spinach beds. Source: ARC.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
296
In order to demonstrate the benefits of 
mulching, a research study was conducted at 
ARC. The researchers used a variety of mulching 
materials in their research, including newspaper, 
black plastic, and grass/hay mulches, all of 
which were found to effectively stop or reduce 
weed growth, possibly as a result of the 
incoming radiation being absorbed. Newspaper 
mulch and grass mulch were found to be just as 
effective at suppressing weeds as black plastic 
mulch at suppressing weeds. When it comes to 
newspaper mulch, the results are consistent, 
with the application of newspaper mulch on 
sweet potato plants suppressing weeds in the 
field. It is recommended to avoid using maize-meal 
sacks as mulch because they degrade over 
time and can pollute the environment. In both 
cases, weeds were allowed to grow beneath 
the mulch and pressure was applied against the 
mulch as it rose upwards, resulting in the maize-
meal bag mulch being torn to pieces. Despite 
the fact that herbicide treatment is effective in 
weed management, there are concerns about 
its limitations, such as the cost of the chemicals 
and the manner in which they are used.

A negative impact of herbicides on soil 
ecosystems and the environment will include 
lowering microbial activity in soils and 
contaminating the water . Natural organic 
mulches, such as newspaper and grass, were 
used in this study to provide an alternative 
weed control technique. These organic mulches 
may be beneficial when degrading into the 
soil to increase soil organic matter . Weeds 
frequently emerge from planting holes, no 
matter how small and insignificant they were 
in terms of number and size, and black plastic 
and newspaper mulches are ineffective at 
completely eliminating them. According to this study, mulching (grass/hay 
and white plastic) improved water use efficiency 
(WUE) while also increasing yield in Swiss chard 
production. If you live in a country with limited 
water resources, like South Africa, water savings 
of up to 65% can make a significant difference 
in the success of a crop's production. For both 
commercial and small-scale farmers, grass/hay 
mulch could be a viable strategy for increasing 
water use efficiency (WUE) and weed control. More research on different colours of plastic 
mulches and organic mulches is needed to 
increase yield and water use efficiency (WUE).

Both organic and inorganic mulches conserved 
water while also lowering electrical consumption 
in proportion to irrigation water consumption, 
resulting in a net water conservation benefit. Comparing white plastic and grass/hay mulches 
to other mulching treatments, white plastic and 
grass/hay mulches significantly increased water 
savings.

3.4 ROTATION OF CROPS 
It is essential to follow crop rotation practices 
when growing vegetables, and it should be 
done on a consistent basis. When it comes to 
crop rotation, the best definition is that it is a 
crop production strategy that involves planting 
different crops in a specific order so that no 
crop is planted on the same piece of land more 
than once every three to four years, preferably 
four to six years (Table 6). Crop rotation is recommended for improving 
soils and conserving resources, but it may have 
the greatest impact on disease levels in the soil 
when used in conjunction with other practices. Many infections can survive after a crop has 
been removed from the soil, according to 
experts (e.g. black-rot of cabbage). Without the practice of rotational cropping, 
the incidence of infestation will undoubtedly 
increase, and the cost of control measures will 
rise in lockstep with it. It also has the potential 
to reduce insect populations and perennial 
weed infestations, which are both detrimental 
to crop production. Perennial weeds, which are 
expected to become a serious problem with 
continued cultivation, are also a concern.

The crops included in the plots shown above 
are as follows:
• Legumes: beans, peas, cowpeas, pigeon 
peas, Bambara
• Brassicas: cabbage, chinese cabbage, kale, 
radish, cauliflower
• Root crops: carrots, onions, beet, sweet 
potato, amadumbi
• Solanaceae: tomato, potato, peppers
• Leafy crops: swiss chard, amaranthus, 
cleome
It is possible to include cucurbits (such as 
pumpkins, squashes, melons, and cucumbers) 
and green mealies at any point in the rotation. Another general rule is to avoid planting an 
underground-bearing crop in the same soil for 
two consecutive seasons if possible. 3.5 BAG SYSTEM
In order to maximise yield per unit area for 
household production, this method of growing 
vegetables is an alternative to traditional 
methods. Plants grown in a bag system grow 
vertically upwards, resulting in more plants 
and yield per unit area than plants grown in a 
traditional system (Figure 17). Table 6 An example of a rotational cropping system with four plots over five years.

Figure 17 Bag system just after transplanting. Source: ARC. This system is recommended for small-scale 
household gardens with a limited supply of 
good-quality soil available. The availability 
of good soil and land/space for household 
vegetable production is increasingly becoming 
a major source of concern. However, there is an 
alternative method of growing vegetables and 
optimising yield per unit area for household 
production that is worth mentioning. Plants 
grown in a bag system grow vertically upward, 
allowing for more efficient use of available 
space/land because of the vertical growth. The 
bag system can be used in places that were 
previously thought to be unsuitable for food 
gardens, such as paved land and balconies, and 
has the potential to save money. The bag has 
the ability to hold water for a longer period 
of time, preventing water and nutrients from 
being washed away into the soil. Because the 
leaves of the plant are not in contact with the 
soil, less effort is required to clean the leaves 
prior to consumption or marketing. It is possible 
to produce organically.

The Swiss chard/spinach in this small bag 
system, when fully planted, can provide a family 
of four with a bunch of spinach once a week for 
a month (Figure 17). Inputs required to plant vegetables in a bag 
system
• Empty maize-meal bag (80, 50 or 25, 12.5 
kg bag
• Water-soluble fertiliser (multi-feed and 
calcium nitrate)
• Growing medium (compost, sawdust or soil 
with very good drainage)
• Watering can
• Seedlings

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
300
Advantages of the bag system 
• It conserves water since there is a minimum 
amount of water draining out of the bag. • It suppresses weeds, no weed control is 
needed. • Plant leaves are free from soil particles 
since plant leaves face upwards. • There is a high yield per unit area, compared 
to growing on a flat area. • Requires less area to produce food (56 
plants m
-2). Disadvantages of the bag system
• Poor drainage medium can have a negative 
effect on moisture distribution and root 
aeration. • Bags need to be supported and kept upright 
for uniform distribution of water . • Maize-meal bag cannot be re-used, with 
time it can tear apart and disintegrate, 
depending on the durability of the bag. 3.6 COVER CROPS
As defined by the USDA, a cover crop is any plant 
population that is grown between the rows of 
another crop, or that is planted between periods 
of regular crop production, with the goal of 
improving soil health and moisture retention, 
preventing soil erosion, and adding nitrogen or 
organic matter to the soil (USDA, 2021). A cover crop is a fundamental and long-lasting 
tool that can be used to manage a variety of 
soil health functions.

It is defined as any type of 
plant that is grown for the purpose of improving 
any number of conditions associated with long-
term agricultural production. a cover crop is a 
crop that is planted to manage soil fertility, soil 
quality, water, weeds, pests and diseases, as well 
as biodiversity and wildlife in an agroecosystem, 
which is an ecological system managed and 
largely shaped by humans to produce food 
across a range of intensities. Agroecosystems 
are ecological systems that are managed and 
largely shaped by humans to produce food 
(Sarah, 2013). Why consider a cover crop? • Cover crops can be used to suppress the 
growth of weeds. In reality, they are capable 
of much more. Some popular cover crops, 
such as oats, rye, and sorghum, produce 
biochemical exudates that are toxic to weed 
seeds and prevent them from germinating.

Figure 18 Bag system (pictured, Ms Hellen Mokitlane).
Source: ARC.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
301
• Cover crops can provide protection against 
root knot nematodes, which are the most 
costly pest in the world. Brassicas produce 
isothiocyanates in their root exudates, 
which are toxic to this invading organism. Furthermore, isothiocyanates have been 
shown to provide protection against some 
of the most destructive fungal diseases. • Cover crops containing brassicas can also 
produce an allelopathic response, which 
can be used to discourage the growth of 
weeds. Several unidentified compounds, 
as well as isothiocyanates, appear to be 
involved in this process. It has long been 
known that the decaying residues of 
crucifers inhibit the germination of weeds 
and the growth of some crops (particularly 
small-seeded crops). Due to the fact that 
weed seeds are typically smaller in size than 
crop seeds, they are at a disadvantage in this 
situation. Growers must be cautious when 
planting small-seeded crops such as lettuce 
after a cover crop dominated by brassicas 
because the seeds will germinate quickly. The breakdown compounds produced 
by mustard as the leaves decompose are 
particularly effective against grasses that 
are a source of concern.

Additionally, rye, 
wheat, and hairy vetch produce compounds 
that discourage the growth of weeds. • In addition, cover crops provide a constant 
supply of glucose to the soil's microbial 
workforce through the roots of the plants. However, this is not the end of the story. When these crops are harvested, they 
provide a tremendous humus-building 
opportunity, as well as all of the benefits 
that accrue as a result of increasing humus 
levels in the soil. Cover cropping has been 
shown to improve soil structure, particularly 
when a cocktail of cover crops is used, 
according to extensive research. • Cover crops have a significant impact on 
erosion reduction. However, at our current 
rate of topsoil loss, we will have no food-
producing soil in just 60 years, making it a 
precious and life-sustaining medium (in all 
contexts). Soil erosion is caused by only 
0.1% of all rainfall events, which accounts 
for 75% of all soil erosion. It was anticipated 
that the residues from no-till farming would 
aid in the prevention of losses due to 
extreme weather, but this has not proven 
to be the case. If you compare living mulch 
from a cover crop with crop residues from 
no-till farming, there is no comparison.

• Cover crops provide a habitat for beneficial 
insects, as well as providing them with 
nectar and pollen. They are becoming 
an increasingly important component of 
the Integrated Pest Management (IPM) 
strategy. While velvet beans are not suitable 
for human consumption, when used as 
a cover crop, they help to increase the 
number of beneficial free-living predatory 
nematodes in the soil, which is beneficial to 
the environment. Recommended cover crops include oats, velvet 
beans, cowpeas, forage sorghum, dry beans, 
soya beans, clover, chickpeas, buckwheat, 
barley, wheat, lupines, lucerne, teff, ryegrass, 
and vetch. Elements affecting the choice of a cover crop
• Know what the desired outcome should be 
• What the farm’s environment is 
• What the farm’s existing soil fertility levels 
are 
• What are the growth patterns and nutrient 
requirements of the farm’s commercial 
crops 
• Need sufficient financial resources to 
implement a cover-cropping program

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual Vegetable production 302 Also, consider the following before a crop is chosen • Climate • Soil type, soil depth, and composition, pH level • Fertility • Existing soil surface covering • Frost patterns and severity • Crop pests and diseases Advantages of cover crops • Contribute to soil nutrients which are then made available to growing crops • Increase soil fertility • Improve soil structure • Reduce soil erosion • Reduce weed pressure • Contribute to breaking pest and disease cycles • Trap nutrients • Increase the organic matter in the soil • Reduce effects of compaction and plough pans • Improved macro- and micro-organism activity in the soil • Lower soil temperatures • Reduction in quantities of pesticides and herbicides used on regular crops Potential disadvantages • Higher direct input costs and management requirements • More competition for soil nutrients and moisture between the cover crops and the regular crops • A potential rise in weed pressure if cover crops are not managed correctly • Increased risk of fire due to the large quantities of dry material left on the land • Increase need for specialized machinery to manage cover crops 3.7 GREEN MANURE They are called "green manure" because they provide nutrients to the soil much

Growing specific plant or crop varieties 
and turning them into the soil to improve the 
overall quality of the soil is referred to as green 
manure. The crop can be harvested and then 
ploughed into the soil, or it can be simply left 
in the ground for an extended period of time 
before being tilled into garden areas (Tilley, 
2020). Examples of a few crops know for green 
manuring are included below. 3.7.1 Sunn hemp (Crotalaria juncea L.)
Do you want a cover crop that will increase 
organic matter, provide nitrogen, grow in low 
fertility sandy soils, and is free of nematodes? Do you want a cover crop that will increase 
organic matter, provide nitrogen, grow in low 
fertility sandy soils, and is free of nematodes? Because of its rapid growth and requirement for 
a relatively short growing season, sunn hemp 
can be an excellent alternative. It thrives in 
well-drained soils with pH levels ranging from 
5-7.5. Sunn hemp has been used extensively 
as a soil improvement or green manure crop 
in the tropics due to its ability to produce large 
amounts of biomass in as little as 60-90 days, 
making it an excellent choice for this application. As a result, it has the potential to increase 
organic matter levels in the environment 
while also sequestering carbon.

Furthermore, 
because it is a legume, it has the ability to fix 
large amounts of nitrogen. A cover crop such 
as sunn hemp can improve soil properties 
by reducing erosion and conserving water 
while also recycling nutrients from the plants 
that grow in it. Suitable for use in hot, humid 
summer rainfall areas (or irrigation). The best 
time to plant is as soon as soil temperatures 
reach 16
oC (October - November). Sow at a rate 
of 40-50 kg ha-1.

4 SOIL IMPROVEMENT AND IRRIGATION
4.1 RAINWATER HARVESTING
Rainwater harvesting is the process of collecting 
rainwater after it has fallen. Storage of water is 
required in order to make it available for use 
later on. Surface runoff of rainwater is being 
generated, collected, stored, and conserved 
in order to mitigate the effects of rainwater 
shortages that occur on a temporary basis. Trapping and holding rain where it falls can help 
to increase the amount of water stored in the 
soil profile over time (see Figure 19). Testing rainwater harvesting and conservation 
techniques for vegetable crops: Case study 
using Swiss chard and Amaranth at ARC
Results of this study indicate that the use of 
simpler tillage techniques such as conventional 
tillage, in conjunction with a wet rainfall 
season or climates with average annual rainfall 
greater than 600 mm, can be sufficient to 
maximise the productivity of Amaranth and 
Swiss chard. When grown under the in-field 
rainwater harvesting technique during a wet 
rainfall season, both amaranth and Swiss chard 
yielded significantly less than when grown 
under normal conditions (see Figure 20 ).

In 
contrast to Swiss chard, this reduction in yield 
was more pronounced in Amaranth, indicating 
that Amaranth is better adapted to high rainfall 
events, in which soil available water exceeds 
crop water requirements. As a result, during drier rainfall seasons, or 
in arid and semi-arid climatic regions where 
annual rainfall is typically less than 600 mm, the 
yield of both Amaranth and Swiss chard is lower 
in comparison to the yield during wetter rainfall 
seasons. Figure 19 Preparing rainwater-harvesting plot and plastic covered catchment area. Source: ARC.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
304
Furthermore, the infield rainwater harvesting 
technique (IRWH) outperformed the tied ridges 
and conventional tillage techniques for both 
crops in terms of yield and quality. According 
to the results, the response of Swiss chard to 
the implementation of the IRWH technique 
was more pronounced than that of Amaranth, 
indicating that Swiss chard has a greater 
demand for water than Amaranth. In addition, 
when comparing Swiss chard and Amaranth, 
the reduction in canopy growth and yield 
caused by water stress was more severe in the 
former crop. The results obtained thus far in this study 
indicate that Amaranth is more tolerant of 
water-logging conditions than Swiss chard and 
that it is, therefore, likely to require a lower 
water supply in order to produce its maximum 
yield. Agro-processing benefits from Amaranth 
cultivation in dry-land conditions during a 
wet season have been demonstrated for 
postharvest agro-processing applications.

The 
crop's response to severe drought conditions (as 
measured by seasonal rainfall received during 
the growing season, as well as the length and 
frequency of dry spells during a dry season) still 
needs to be assessed in order to provide a more 
detailed assessment of the crop's performance 
when grown on dry land. Figure 20 Rainwater harvesting and mulching trial of Swiss chard and Amaranth 
planted in November 2015. Source: ARC.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
305
Investigating the interactive effects of water 
and nitrogen on yield, nutritional water 
productivity and metabolomics of nightshade 
(Solanum retroflexum) at ARC
The results revealed that nightshade responded 
in a variety of ways to different irrigation and 
nitrogen (N) treatment regimes (Figure 21 ). In 
comparison to the well-irrigated treatments 
(66% and 100% FC), the 33% FC (field capacity) 
treatment produced lower fresh biomass yield 
but had higher water productivity, indicating 
that increasing water application up to an 
optimum level could result in increased biomass 
yield. Given that nitrogen is a highly mobile 
nutrient in the soil and that irrigation water is 
applied on a frequent basis, the reduction in 
fresh biomass yield observed with the 100% 
FC treatment could be attributed to leaching 
out of N beyond the root zone. Fresh biomass 
yield was significantly higher with the moderate 
irrigation treatment (66% FC), and crop water 
productivity was highest with the rain-fed 
irrigation treatment (66% FC).

Because the trial 
was conducted in open field conditions and rain 
had to be factored into the soil water balance 
equation, there are no statistically significant 
differences in water use between 33% FC and 
66% FC. This is due to the fact that the trial was 
conducted in open field conditions and rain 
had to be factored into the soil water balance 
equation. The highest average Zn content was obtained 
under rain-fed irrigation treatments, and the 
highest average N content was obtained at a 
rate of 100 kg ha
-1, with an average yield of 75 kg 
ha-1. This study found that the lowest Zn content 
was found in the 100% FC and 200 kg ha -1 N 
treatments. This suggests that maintaining 100% 
FC soil moisture content while increasing your N 
application rate from 150 kg ha
-1 will result in a 
decrease in Zn content in the leaves. Fe content 
of nightshade under rain-fed and irrigated 
conditions was identical when fertilised with 0 
kg N ha
-1 of nitrogen. When fertilised with 150 
kg ha-1 of nitrogen, the lowest iron (Fe) content 
was obtained and the values decreased sharply; 
however, when fertilised with 150 kg ha
-1 of 
nitrogen, the lowest Fe content was obtained 
and the values decreased sharply.
