4.2 INTERCROPPING STUDY AT 
ARC
Comparative analysis of advantages provided 
by legumes to native non-leguminous 
indigenous leafy vegetables when grown in 
intercropping and crop rotation systems. The 
majority of smallholder farmers have depleted 
their soils of nutrients in large quantities 
without replenishing them, either through the 
application of sufficient manure or the use of 
chemical fertilisers. However, nitrogen and 
phosphorus are essential in the soil, and farmers 
will not be able to maximise their yields if these 
nutrients are not present. As a result, improving 
soil fertility is a critical component of increasing 
crop productivity in Africa, which is currently 
experiencing low production trends. Because 
of the reduced use of chemicals, this can be 
made more affordable for smallholder farmers 
by intercropping leafy indigenous legumes 
with non-legumes and, as a result, making the 
environment more sustainable. In general, the study's goal was to generate 
knowledge about the cultivation of indigenous 
leafy vegetables by smallholder farmers through 
the use of intercropping and rotational farming 
systems.

The following objectives were established in 
order to achieve the study's overall goal:
Aims: To determine the impact of legume 
(cowpea) and non-legume (Amaranth) 
intercropping nutritional values on soil 
fertility and crop productivity at various levels 
of nutrition (25, 50, 75, and 100% of the 
recommendation) (Figure 22). • To determine the level of microbial activity 
in legumes (acid and alkaline phosphatase 
activity)
• To determine the amount of nitrogen fixed 
by legumes
The overall goal of the study is to generate 
new knowledge about intercropping and crop 
rotation systems for smallholder farmers who 
cultivate indigenous vegetables. Figure 22 Cowpea and amaranth planted (intercropping) at ARC-Roodeplaat. Source: ARC.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
307
The study's specific objectives include: We will 
be measuring the nutrient concentrations in 
plant and soil samples in order to assess the 
benefits of intercropping and crop rotation of 
indigenous vegetables grown in South Africa. 
This has not yet been looked into further . 
Non-legumes are expected to benefit from 
intercropping and crop rotation, which are both 
recommended. When legumes are grown with 
little or no fertilisation, higher nitrogen fixation 
is expected. Using indigenous vegetables, the 
project will demonstrate the beneficial effects 
of intercropping and crop rotation on the 
plants. The project will be implemented in rural 
communities in six South African provinces by 
the end of the five-year period (Limpopo, North 
West, Mpumalanga, Kwa Zulu Natal, Gauteng 
and Eastern Cape).

4.3 VERMICULTURE
Improvement of indigenous vegetable 
production among smallholder farmers in 
South Africa through the use of vermiculture 
products to improve soil quality is being 
pursued. Agricultural productivity has increased 
in part as a result of the depletion of natural 
resources as a result of the farming systems 
currently in use. Consequently, an urgent call 
is needed to reverse the trend by encouraging 
farmers to adopt farming methods that are 
environmentally friendly and have a long-
term benefit to the environment. It is difficult 
for farmers to manage intensive farming 
operations on a sustainable basis because they 
lack the necessary knowledge, resources, and 
skills. In turn, inappropriate technologies and 
unsustainable practises are employed, which 
contribute to the depletion of natural resources 
and the pollution of the environment in which 
they are used. It is possible to significantly reduce 
the problem by using soil organic amendment 
products such as vermicompost, which is an 
organic fertiliser rich in NKP and micronutrients 
and can be applied to the soil. Vermicompost 
also contains beneficial soil microbes, such 
as nitrogen-fixing and phosphate-solubilizing 
bacteria, as well as actinomycetes, which help 
the soil to function properly.

Vermicompost is 
more nutritive when lime is added to it, and 
it has made a significant contribution to the 
improvement of plant growth. The project's goal is to persuade smallholder 
farmers to use organic soil amendments 
instead of chemical fertilisers in their efforts to 
increase the production of native vegetables. Utilizing such practises will result in an increase 
in fertility, water holding capacity, and organic 
matter in your soils. More sustainable farming 
will be achieved in the long run, contributing 
to increased crop productivity, increased food 
security, and poverty alleviation while also 
caring for the environment and conserving 
natural resources. 4.4 IRRIGATION SYSTEMS
Even though there are many different types of 
irrigation systems available, no single irrigation 
system is optimally suited for every situation 
and every crop. The most important factors to 
consider when deciding on the type of irrigation 
system to use are discussed in greater detail 
below. 4.4.1 Influence of environmental factors in 
the selection of the irrigation system
When deciding on the irrigation system 
to be used, the influence of agro-climatic 
conditions must be taken into consideration.

Despite the fact that taking into account agro-
climatic conditions is critical in the selection 
of an irrigation method and the effective 
implementation of that method, irrigation

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
308
methods are frequently selected and promoted 
without taking this into consideration. Thus, 
the following checklist identifies agricultural-
climatic factors that should be considered when 
determining the most appropriate irrigation 
method:
Soil type
Sandy soils have a low water storage capacity 
and a high infiltration rate, making them ideal 
for irrigation. As a result, they require more 
frequent but smaller irrigation applications, 
which is particularly important when the 
sandy soil is also shallow. Rather than using a 
surface irrigation system, sprinklers or localised 
irrigation are more appropriate in these 
situations. If the soil is loam or clay, any of the 
three irrigation methods can be used; however, 
surface irrigation is the most commonly used 
method. Clay soils with low infiltration rates are 
ideal for surface irrigation because they allow 
water to pass through quickly. A sprinkler system 
or localised irrigation is recommended when 
there are several different soil types present in 
one irrigation scheme because they will ensure 
a uniform distribution of water throughout the 
entire system. For this soil type, drip irrigation 
may also be an option to consider .

Slope
When dealing with steeper or undulating terrain, 
sprinkler or localised irrigation is frequently 
preferred over surface irrigation because it 
requires little or no land levelling. Crops that are 
grown on terraces are an exception to this rule. On a sloping terrace, drip irrigation can also be 
an effective method of watering the plants. Climate
Strong winds will have a negative impact on the 
uniform distribution of irrigation water as well 
as on the efficiency of sprinkler irrigation, which 
will be reduced. When there is a lot of wind, 
localized/drip irrigation methods or surface 
irrigation methods are preferred. Surface 
irrigation may be preferable in areas where 
supplementary irrigation is required because 
of the flexibility and adaptability of sprinkler or 
localised irrigation systems to varying irrigation 
demands in these areas. Water availability
Surface irrigation has a lower efficiency than a 
sprinkler and localized/drip irrigation, which is 
why these methods are more commonly used. As a result, when water is in short supply, these 
methods are preferred.

Nevertheless, it should 
be remembered that the efficiency of a method 
is just as much a function of the duration of 
the crop cover, the evaporative demand, and 
the standard of water management as it is 
of the method itself. When irrigation water 
contains dissolved salts, localised irrigation is 
particularly advantageous because less water is 
applied to the soil than with surface application 
methods, resulting in better water quality. Sprinkler systems are generally more efficient 
in the leaching of salts than surface irrigation 
methods, according to research. 4.4.2 Types of irrigation systems and a 
comparison of the different systems
• Systems that are permanent (drip, micros 
and permanent sprinkler systems). • Systems that can be moved by hand (such 
as the dragline and overhead sprinklers) are 
available. • For flood irrigation, hosepipes and furrow 
irrigation are frequently used methods. Hand watering systems, bucket irrigation 
systems, drip irrigation systems, as well as 
sprayers that use portable hoses, are all 
examples of irrigation systems that have been 
developed specifically for small garden plots 
(draglines) (Table 7).

4.4.2.1 Movable sprayers (Draglines)
• Labor intensive. • The spray radius will seldom exceed 8 m. • The application rate is high and if the 
sprayer is left in one position for too long, 
water will run off and not infiltrate the soil. 4.4.2.2 Micro sprayers
• Micro-irrigation systems are used when 
water is sprayed directly onto the plants and 
can increase infestation by fungal diseases.

• Micro-sprayer nozzles have different delivery 
rates and patterns of water distribution and 
should be selected according to the crop 
requirements. • Evaporation losses in the air and from the 
soil surface can be significant. • Normally these systems are permanently 
installed for the duration of the crop’s 
growing season and can be removed after 
harvesting to be re-used for new planting. 4.4.2.3 Dripper irrigation
• Drip irrigation is the most water-efficient 
way of irrigation and is utilised when the 
water supply is limited. • Irrigation efficiency is good as water is 
placed within the root zone. • Runoff and evaporation are limited to the 
minimum. • This system can be adapted to suit a wide 
variety of crops and growing conditions. • The use of drip irrigation is limited mainly 
because of additional costs for filters and 
control systems. • Drip systems are permanently installed for 
the duration of the crop’s growing season 
and can be removed after harvesting to be 
re-used for new planting. • Drippers are a high maintenance system 
and need good management skills.

4.4.2.4 Flood irrigation
• The slow soaking of the water into the soil 
will wet the soil evenly to the depth you 
want, but there is a danger of applying too 
much water this way. • Furrow irrigation is most successful on level 
ground and with soil that is not very sandy. Make the furrows before planting, making 
them up to 30 cm wide and 15 cm deep, 
and make them as level as possible. • Hoses can also be used for hand watering 
and many nozzles have been designed to 
deliver a shower of water rather than a jet 
of water . Some vegetables, particularly lettuce and 
potatoes, have sparse root systems that do not 
make good contact with the soil moisture that is 
available in their root-depth zone as a result of 
this.

Application of sufficient water to bring the 
soil moisture content in the effective rooting 
zone of the crop up to the field capacity level. Irrigating vegetables should not be delayed until 
they show signs of wilting or develop color or 
texture changes that indicate that they are not 
growing properly (see Table 8).

Even though irrigation systems are expensive 
to install, sub-surface or normal drip systems 
reduce the need to retrieve pipes and may 
become more common as water becomes 
scarcer in South Africa as a result of the drought 
there is currently. Drip irrigation systems, while 
having the lowest water and electricity efficiency 
of the various types of irrigation systems, also 
have the advantage of lowering the cost of 
cultivation. It keeps weeds under control by

area.

4.5 SOIL MOISTURE
After rainfall or irrigation, all the soil pore spaces 
are filled with water . The air is displaced and the 
soil is water-saturated. If the rain or irrigation 
were to continue, then surface pooling or runoff 
would occur. The rate at which a soil can absorb 
water is influenced by its texture and structure. All sands and loamy sands tend to have a low 
water holding capacity. Loams and clay have 
a medium water holding capacity, while very 
fine sandy loam, silt loams, peats, and any soil 
with a high water table have high water-holding 
capacities. Clay soils often have a large amount 
of water within them, but it is not available for 
plant use. A shovel will serve to obtain a soil sample from 
shallow soils or when a shallow-rooted crop 
is being grown. A soil auger or soil tube is 
necessary to draw samples from greater depths 
in the root zones. Squeeze the soil sample in 
the hand and compare its behavior with those 
of the soils listed to get a rough idea of its 
moisture content. During rainfall or irrigation, water seeps into the 
soil pores and completely fills them with water . The air has been forced out of the space, and the 
soil has become saturated with liquid.

If the rain 
or irrigation continues, it is possible that surface 
pooling or runoff will occur. It is important to 
take into account the texture and structure of 
the soil when it comes to soil absorption. Water holding capacity is generally low in all 
sands and loamy sands, regardless of their 
composition. Clay and loam have medium 
water-holding capacities, whereas very fine 
sandy loam, silt loam, peat bogs, and other soils 
with a high water table have high water-holding 
capacities. Clay soils frequently contain a large 
amount of water, but this water is not available 
to plants for use as a source of nutrition because 
it is not available to them. For the purpose of collecting soil samples 
from shallow soils or when growing crops with 
shallow roots, a shovel must be used. In order 
to collect samples from deeper in the root zone, 
it is necessary to use a soil auger or a soil tube 
to collect the samples. You can get an idea of its 
moisture content in a rough sense by squeezing 
and comparing the soil sample in your hand to 
the behavior of the soils listed in Table 9.

4.6 WATERING TECHNIQUES
From the time of planting until the time of 
harvest, food crops require a consistent supply 
of water . If you give your plants too little water, 
they will begin to wilt and, if the situation is not 
corrected, they will die eventually. Giving plants 
too much water may cause the roots to become 
drowned, resulting in the plants ceasing to grow 
or dying. Proper (correct) watering is something 
of an art – the best way to learn how often and 
how much to water your crops is through trial 
and error in the field. The frequency with which you water your 
crops is determined by the types of crops you 
are growing, the age and size of the plants, the 
type of soil, and the weather . In order to keep 
their root zones moist, young seedlings with 
small shallow roots that are located in the top 
layer of the soil will require frequent watering – 
sometimes as frequently as twice or three times 
per day. Crops with deep roots in the soil (such 
as pigeon pea) may only require watering once 
a week or only once every two weeks. Watering frequency is determined by the 
amount of clay, sand, silt, and organic matter 
present in the soil. Clay is the most common 
type of soil.

Clay soils retain a large amount of 
water and release it slowly, whereas sandy soils 
retain a small amount of water and release it 
quickly. The weather also has an impact on how 
frequently the crop needs to be watered. Cool, 
cloudy weather allows any soil to retain moisture 
for a longer period of time than it would in hot, 
dry weather . In a few places, rainfall during the 
growing season is sufficient to meet all of the 
water requirements. It is necessary for water to reach the root zone 
of the plants in sufficient quantities. The depth 
to which a given amount of water will penetrate 
is determined by the soil type. Water applied to 
a square metre of land at a rate of 25 litres per 
square metre will wet a sandy soil to a depth of 
30 cm, while clay soil will be wet to a depth of 
10 cm to 15 cm.. 4.7 WATER REQUIREMENTS OF

VEGETABLE CROPS 
Vegetables require a consistent supply of 
water from the time they are planted until 
they are harvested, and they cannot be grown 
successfully in rain-fed conditions. The following 
are only general guidelines that should be 
tailored to your specific plot or garden based on 
the season and local conditions:
• Seedlings: The critical time is between 
sowing and the emergence of the seedling. 
At all times the soil in contact with the seed 
must be moist.
• Transplants: Watering before and after 
transplanting is essential, particularly in hot 
weather 
• Leafy crops: Leafy crops such as Brassicas 
(e.g. Swiss chard, Cabbage) generally need 
about 25 litres per square meter soil a 
week and should be actively growing from 
the time they are sown or transplanted. 
Any lack in water supply can affect yield 
and quality and during summer it may be 
necessary to irrigate twice a week 
• Root crops (e.g. Potatoes, carrots, sweet 
potato): The average weekly water 
requirement is between 10-15 litres per 
square meter during the first month after 
planting. From one month after planting 
until plants approach maturity 30 litres per 
square meter will be adequate. Regular 
watering must be maintained in the

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
315
absence of rain and it is important to ensure 
that water penetrates deeply into the soil. Shallow watering discourages good root 
development. Potatoes need additional 
water when tubers start forming. • Other crops (e.g. green beans and other 
legumes, cucurbits, Solanaceae): The 
average weekly water requirement is 25 
litre per square meter but varies according 
to the stage of development. Water-efficient vegetables (e.g. cowpeas, 
amaranth, pigeon peas and Bambara). These 
crops are known to be tolerant to drought and 
can be grown under rainfed conditions if the 
rain is well distributed. 4.8 WATER-SAVING TECHNIQUES
Watering slowly, deeply, and infrequently is the 
key to success. Plants with shallow root systems 
are encouraged to grow as a result of frequent 
shallow watering. • Use drip or trickle irrigation. This method 
wets the soil slowly, allowing for slow, 
deep penetration. Up to 60% of the 
irrigation, water can be saved using drip 
vs. conventional sprinkler irrigation. A 
perforated hose placed upside down (with 
holes facing down) makes a very good 
temporary drip irrigation system. • Water at low application rates.

If water runs 
off the soil surface or forms puddles, the 
water application rate is too high and water 
is wasted. • Irrigate at night or in the early morning 
when the temperatures are cooler and 
the humidity is higher . There will be less 
evaporation. • Plant vegetable plants at optimal spacing. Using this method decreases the amount 
of water needed per unit area, and reduces 
evaporation from the soil surface. • Use drought-tolerant (water conserving) 
plants or varieties. Separate water-
conserving plants from water-demanding 
plants. Group plants with similar water 
requirements together . Other methods that can be used to supplement 
water in the garden will be discussed. 4.8.1 Use of greywater 
Use the wastewater, usually referred to as 
greywater (all the non-toilet wastewater 
produced in the average household, including 
the water from bathtubs, showers, sinks, 
washing machines, and dishwashers) (Figure 
23): 
• Apply the greywater directly to the soil 
surface. Do not use an overhead sprinkler, 
or allow the recycled water to splash off the 
soil and be exposed to the above-ground 
portion of the plants. • Rotate applications of greywater with 
fresh water . The freshwater will help leach 
out any soil contaminants that might be 
building up.

4.8.2 Rooftop water harvesting
Rooftop catchments and cistern storage have 
been the basis of domestic water supply in many 
countries. Collect rainwater from the roofs of 
buildings into tanks as follows (see Figure 24).

4.8.3 In-field rainwater harvesting
The principle is to transfer run-off water from 
a land area that is not cropped to supplement 
rainfall received directly on the area where crops 
are grown (Figure 25). This is more relevant for 
areas where drought is experienced and rainfall 
is irregular .

There are two types of catchment areas:
• Catchment strips within the boundary of 
the cultivated area are altered to direct run-
off to cultivated plots
• In the external areas (for example, the 
playground and the roads), water is diverted 
to the cultivated areas. Take, for example, 
the incline of the school grounds. Water will 
be directed to the school garden if trenches 
are dug to direct it. Plant something, such as 
fruit trees, in a furrow that will receive this 
water . This method is not recommended 
for use in shallow soils or areas prone to 
landslides, among other things
Water collected from trenches on large school 
grounds can be collected in a simple dam 
structure and then used for furrow irrigation. This method is particularly useful for large 
school grounds. It is impossible to prevent droughts from 
occurring, but interventions and preparedness 
for droughts can help (i) better prepared to 
cope with drought; (ii) develop more resilient 
crops and ecosystems; (iii) improve resilience 
to recover from drought, and (iv) mitigate 
the consequences of droughts.

Drought 
preparedness strategies include: (a) shifting 
agricultural systems to more arid regions; (b) 
climate-proofing rainfall-based systems; and (c) 
introducing more efficient irrigation systems to 
increase efficiency. 4.9 INSTRUMENTS MAKING

IRRIGATED SYSTEMS MORE

EFFICIENT
Tensiometer: Measures the water status in the 
soil (increased reading indicates that the soil is 
too dry and vice versa).
Chameleon: Measures the water status in the 
soil.
The above instruments use the same principle 
where they will give a certain meaningful 
reading, but the chameleon is much more 
farmer user friendly (blue, red and green lights 
will light up depending on the soil water status).
