5 SELECTED DROUGHT TOLERANT VEGETABLES
Drought is one of the most serious 
environmental threats to agriculture around the 
world. Drought affects vegetables more than 
other crops. Because of this, there has been a 
renewed emphasis on identifying and improving 
underutilised indigenous and traditional crops 
for drought tolerance (Mabhaudhi, 2009). Crops that have either originated in South Africa 
or have become "indigenised" over many years 
(>10 decades) of cultivation, as well as natural 
and farmer selection within South Africa, are 
examples of underutilised indigenous and 
traditional crops (Schippers, 2002, 2006). 5.1 PEARL MILLET
South Africa's pearl millet is an example of 
an indigenous cereal that can be found in the 
northern and western regions of the country. Small-holder farmers in South Africa produce 
the majority of the pearl millet that is consumed 
by the country's population. There are some 
areas where it is only grown commercially as 
forage for animal consumption, but this is not 
the case everywhere. Despite the fact that pearl 
millet can grow in a wide variety of soils ranging 
from clay to loam to deep sand, it performs 
best when grown in deep, well-drained soil.

Pearl millet is a crop that is easy to cultivate 
and can be grown in arid and semi-arid regions 
where water availability is a constraint on crop 
production. A "high energy" cereal, pearl millet is 
distinguished by having a higher oil content than 
maize, as well as higher levels of vitamin A and 
protein. Pearl millet is less susceptible to pests 
and diseases when compared to other staple 
grains such as maize, wheat, and sorghum. 5.2 TARO (COLOCASIA ESCULENTA 
(L.) OR AMADUMBE 
Taro is a member of the Araceae family of plants. Taro leaves and corms are both edible and 
contain a high concentration of carbohydrate, 
vitamin A and C, as well as protein. Taro is an 
indigenized crop grown in South Africa, where 
it is neglected and underutilised, as are many 
other traditional crops. TARO's leaves and 
corms are both edible and contain a significant 
amount of carbohydrate as well as vitamins A 
and C and protein. It is most prevalent along the 
coasts of KwaZulu-Natal and the Eastern Cape. Taro production has increased as a result of 
improved access to niche markets, according to 
the USDA. Local studies into drought tolerance 
and water use of some of the landraces currently 
in production, on the other hand, have not been 
conducted in sufficient numbers.

It is possible 
that taro production and commercialization will 
be expanded beyond their current levels as a 
result of improved information availability. 5.3 WILD MUSTARD (BRASSICA 
JUNCEA L.)
Wild mustard is a crop that is indigenous 
to South Africa and is a member of the 
Brassicaceae family of plants. There are many 
different environmental conditions under which 
it can be grown, and it has a significant impact 
on the nutritional and economic well-being of 
rural South Africans. In addition to providing 
essential vitamins and trace elements (iron and 
calcium), wild mustard also contains a variety of 
other nutrients necessary for good health. The 
oil and protein content of the seeds is high, but 
this is dependent on the environment in which

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
320
they are grown. In a short period of time, this 
plant establishes itself, achieving maximum 
ground cover, which serves as a stress-relieving 
mechanism, particularly in water-stressed 
environments. 5.4 WILD MELON (CITRULLUS 
IANATUS L.) 
Wild melon is a crop that is indigenous to 
Southern Africa. The Kalahari Desert is thought 
to be the origin of this plant's existence. There, 
the ancestral melon, known as the Tsamma 
melon, grows in its natural habitat. It is a vine-
like plant or climber that bears edible fruits and 
leaves, and it is native to Asia. Wild watermelons 
were extremely bitter in the beginning, but this 
was overcome through selective breeding for 
improved palatability. Wild melon is a member of the Cucurbitaceae 
family, which includes cucumbers. It is cultivated 
all over the world as a staple food, with edible 
seed and flesh, as well as for animal feed and 
animal bedding. The skin is used for pickling 
and the extraction of pectin from the fruit. Seeds have the potential to be a good source of 
protein. It is also considered to be a species that 
is underutilised.

Despite the fact that wild melon is considered 
drought tolerant, water stress during the 
formation of the vines that will bear flowers and 
fruits can have a negative impact on the amount 
of fruit produced. In addition, the quality of the 
seeds is a significant obstacle to the successful 
production of wild melon. 5.5 SWEET POTATO
Sweet potato is still an important root crop, 
and it has the inherent ability to produce more 
edible energy than most major food crops, 
which makes it a valuable source of energy. It is well-suited for production on marginal 
lands and has the potential to be a significant 
contributor to food security. It is impossible 
to overstate the importance of orange-fleshed 
sweet potato varieties in preventing and treating 
vitamin A deficiency. It is recommended that 
the production of orange-fleshed sweet potato 
varieties be encouraged in areas where vitamin A 
deficiency is prevalent. In South Africa's tropical 
and subtropical regions, where warm weather 
prevails, it is possible to grow this crop and 
have it available all year round, despite the fact 
that drought poses a challenge for staple crops 
such as cereals.

Wet weather in subtropical 
environments causes rain-fed crops to be 
subjected to water stress at all growth stages, 
which is especially true during the growing 
season. It has the advantage of being drought 
tolerant once established, which is an added 
bonus. Therefore, its yield potential is generally 
higher than that of popular staple crops in South 
Africa, such as maize. Farmers who grow sweet 
potatoes in areas prone to drought stress will 
benefit from the higher nutritional value of the 
crop, in addition to the higher yield advantage 
that sweet potatoes provide. It is also simple 
to obtain sweet potato because the vines 
and roots can be easily stored or multiplied, 
making it readily available. Despite the fact that 
sweet potato has been identified as a potential 
food security crop for dryland production, 
there are still challenges that may prevent it 
from contributing fully to food security in the 
future. A greater emphasis should be placed 
on participatory research and extension in 
order to educate smallholder farmers about the 
importance of sweet potato as a major crop for 
subsistence and rural economic development.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
321
5.6 SPINACH (SWISS CHARD)
5.6.1 Introduction
In this document, the term spinach will be used 
to refer to Swiss chard. Despite the fact that 
Swiss chard has a similar appearance to spinach, 
it produces much broader leaves with thicker 
stems and contains significantly higher levels of 
vitamin A. Even though spinach itself contains 
a higher concentration of calcium (Ca), it does 
so in an oxalate form that is not suitable for 
children. Swiss chard has become increasingly 
popular as a result of this, as well as the low 
yield of spinach. It is a member of the beetroot 
family of vegetables.
The crop is grown on an annual basis for its 
nutritionally dense and luscious leaves. Both the 
leaves and the stems (petiole) are edible and 
can be prepared in a variety of ways. With the 
exception of vitamins, it also contains significant 
amounts of readily available minerals, with iron 
being the most important of these minerals.
The crop is relatively simple to cultivate and can 
be harvested on a regular basis due to its regular 
harvesting. Average yields range between 8-10 
t ha
-1, with a maximum yield of 15 t ha-1.

Spinach can be used to do the following:
• Cooking with other vegetables such as 
potatoes, onions and tomatoes
• Cooking with minced meat (green meat)
• Can be used in stir-fry recipes
• Can be sun-dried and be stored for later use
5.6.2 Soil requirements
Fertile, well-drained soils are required for the 
production of spinach. The best soil for this 
plant is a sandy loam, but it also grows well in 
loam to clay soil with a pH of 6.0-6.8. 5.6.3 Climatic requirements
Despite the fact that it is a cool-weather crop, 
it can also be grown during the hot summer 
months. Some cultivars, on the other hand, 
may experience bolting as a result of this. The 
optimal temperature ranges between 16- 24oC. The best time to plant in Gauteng is from the 
middle of February to the beginning of April, 
and from August to the end of October . 5.6.4 Cultivars
Ford Hook Giant (FHG) and Lucullus are the 
two most widely planted spinach cultivars. In contrast to FHG, which is more resistant to 
‘bolting' and therefore better suited for summer 
production, Lucullus produces higher yields 
and grows at a faster rate. In addition, there 
is a third cultivar, Rhubarb (which is reddish in 
color), which produces good yields.

5.6.5 Crop rotation
It is not recommended to plant the crop on 
the same plot of land year after year because 
this can result in the accumulation of pests 
and diseases in the soil. Other vegetables such 
as pumpkin, beans and peas are good to plant 
alongside the crop. Other vegetables to plant 
alongside the crop include lettuce, tomatoes, 
potatoes and cabbage. 5.6.6 Soil preparation
It is important to clean the area intended for 
planting at least a month before removing weeds 
and grasses, just as it is with the production of 
any other vegetable. To work the soil, you can 
use a fork to help you out. This practice can aid 
in the prevention of pests and diseases from 
infecting the newly planted crops in the future.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
322
Before beginning the preparation process, 
the soil must be thoroughly watered. This will 
ensure that the soil is loose, which will improve 
drainage, root penetration, and aeration of the 
soil. Using a plough or fork, loosen the soil to the 
proper depth. In addition to fertiliser, spinach 
necessitates a substantial amount of compost 
or manure. When using direct sowing, make sure that the 
seedbed is well prepared and that it has a very 
fine structural structure. Large clods will prevent 
the germination of seeds. 5.6.7 Planting method and spacing
It is possible to plant seedlings in rows with a 
spacing of 10-20 cm between plants and 25-35 
cm between rows, depending on the variety. Alternatively, seeds can be directly sown in 
shallow rows or furrows. Furrows should be 
approximately 2-3 cm deep when made with a 
spade or rake. Sow seeds 2cm apart and cover 
with soil using a spade or a rake to ensure good 
coverage. When growing crops in hot weather, the soil 
must be watered immediately after sowing 
and properly mulched with a thin layer of grass 
(mulching). It is necessary to keep the soil moist 
until the seeds have germinated, which takes 
approximately 7-8 days.

Remember to remove 
the grass after 5-6 days to avoid seedlings that 
are too long and leggy. These plants are heavy 
at the top and are susceptible to damage from 
the sun. It is critical to thin out the plants to a distance of 
10cm between them no later than 3 weeks after 
the seedlings have emerged. 5.6.8 Fertilisation
Before planting, apply a fertiliser containing the 
ratio 2:3:2 (22) Zn. Apply 90 g m
-2 and work into 
the top 10 cm of the soil with a fork. Topdressing is extremely important because 
spinach requires a lot of nitrogen in order 
to produce high-quality, broad leaves and, 
consequently, high yields. When planting or transplanting, a second 
topdressing should be applied 8 weeks after 
the first, especially in lighter soil types. Caution 
should be exercised when applying the 
limestone ammonium nitrate (LAN) fertiliser 
directly to the plants, as this will cause the plants 
to burn. As soon as the fertiliser is applied, it 
must be thoroughly mixed into the top 2 cm of 
the soil with a fork or a grob to ensure proper 
incorporation. Take care not to damage the 
shallow roots, which are particularly vulnerable 
during the early stages of growth. After the 
fertiliser has been worked into the soil, make 
sure to water the plants immediately.

5.6.9 Water requirements
Spinach has a very shallow root system; therefore 
the soil must be kept moist throughout the 
active growing season of the crop. One or two 
light irrigation periods is better than one very 
heavy irrigation period because of the shallow 
root system.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
323
5.6.10 Harvesting
The crop must be harvested on a regular basis in 
order to stimulate regrowth, increase yield, and 
ensure that it does not bolt. It is only necessary 
to remove the outer leaves with a sharp knife, 
30-50 mm above the soil, in order to prevent 
the plant from growing back. Take care not to 
damage the newly formed buds. If the leaves 
are not used immediately, they can be bunched 
together and placed in water to keep them fresh 
for a longer period of time, if necessary. 5.6.11 Storage
Because of the extremely high transpiration 
rate of the broad leaves, the keeping quality 
is extremely poor in this variety. You can store 
spinach in the refrigerator for up to 6-8 days if 
you have the proper storage facilities. 5.6.12 Disease control measures
• Practice crop rotation
• Remove infected plants from the field and 
burn or bury it
• Remove all plant residues from the field 
after harvesting
• No registered chemicals for disease control 
of spinach
• Fertilize plants well
• Avoid over-watering
• Water plants early so that leaves can dry 
before nightfall
5.6.13 Pests
Aphids feed on plant juices and are known to 
spread viral diseases. Regular scouting is required for control.

Using 
soapy water, saturate the surface (Sunlight Liquid 
diluted in water). Phosdrin and Mevinphos are 
two pesticides that have been approved for use. Cutworms normally hibernate beneath the 
surface of the ground during the day and 
emerge at night to feed on young seedlings by 
cutting them down to the ground. Regular scouting is required for control. Make 
use of cutworm bait. Red spider mite: Stippled areas on the leaves 
are an early sign of an infestation of this mite. As a result of the chlorotic condition of the 
leaves, webs appear on the leaves and stems 
in severe cases. Regular spider mite scouting 
is extremely important in the control of spider 
mites. Using soapy water, saturate the surface 
(Sunlight Liquid diluted in water). Infestations of nematodes can be identified by 
the presence of swellings on the roots. Crop rotation is used to keep things under 
control. Planting marigolds, mustard, and 
rapeseed in between rows. Fallowing. Solarization of the soil. Nemacur and EDB are 
two pesticides that have been approved for use. 5.7 AMARANTH (AMARANTHUS 
SPP)
5.7.1 Origin and distribution
Amaranth thrives in warm climates and requires 
little irrigation.

In South Africa, it is rarely 
cultivated due to the misconception that it 
grows naturally, despite the fact that it has the 
potential to be developed as a cultivated crop 
there. Amaranth leaves are high in protein, 
vitamins, and minerals, making them a nutritious 
addition to any diet. Because of its nutritional 
benefits and ability to adapt to a variety of 
environmental conditions, it is considered 
a promising crop for cultivation in marginal, 
arid, and semi-arid regions worldwide. It can 
grow in a wide variety of soils and can tolerate

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
324
pH levels ranging from 4.5-8. In addition, it is 
tolerant of moderate salinity, which means that 
it can be grown in semi-arid climates and areas 
prone to salinity stress, such as deserts. Despite 
the fact that amaranth can withstand adverse 
conditions, irrigation and fertilisation will 
increase both fresh and dry mass in the crop. 5.7.2 Production level: South Africa
Despite the fact that amaranth is not typically 
planted in South Africa, it does appear as 
a volunteer crop after the first rains and is 
harvested from the wild. In South Africa, 
the reasons for cultivating this plant are 
not particularly diverse, with the primary 
motivations being food security for the 
household and replenishment of the country's 
seed bank. It is not known how much Amaranth 
is produced at any given time. A recent study 
has found that under cultivated conditions, 
Amaranth can produce fresh leaf yields of up to 
40 t ha
-1, according to the researchers. The yield 
of grain Amaranth is highly variable, with 1 000 
kg ha
-1 considered to be a good yield in some 
areas.

5.7.3 Major production areas
The provinces of Limpopo, the North West, 
Mpumalanga, and KwaZulu-Natal are the main 
producers of amaranth in South Africa, with the 
rest of the country following closely behind. 5.7.4 Cultivars
Amaranthus has a vast range of morphological 
variability among cultivars (Figure 26 ), even 
within specific Amaranthus species. • Amaranthus cruentus
• hybridus (Africa)
• spinosus (Africa)
• caudatus (America)
• thunbergii (America)
5.7.5 Description of the mature plant
Amaranth species are erect or spreading 
annuals with a rough or prickly appearance 
that can be found in a variety of colours. Grain 
amaranths are available in a variety of colours 
for the flowers, leaves, and stems, but maroon 
or crimson colouring is common in all three 
plant parts. Some varieties have green flowers, 
while others have golden flowers. When they 
are in full bloom, some of the deep crimson 
varieties can be quite eye-catching. Because the 
plant grows at different rates depending on the 
species, growth habitat, and environment, its 
height can range between 0.33-2 m. Figure 26 Amaranth cultivars (A) Amaranthus retroflexus (B) Amaranthus tricolor (C) Amaranthus 
hybridus (D) Amaranthus cruentus. Source: ARC.

A B C D

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
325
Stems
Reddish-tinged, erect, occasionally ascending, 
branched, with linear marks on the surface, 
and hairless to moderately pubescent 
with multicellular hairs; erect, occasionally 
ascending, branched, with linear marks on the 
surface. Leaves
The leaves are variable in size and colour, 
ranging from green to purplish, and are borne 
on slender stalks. According to the species, 
these are alternate, usually simple, with entire 
margins and distinct markings on the underside. Flowers
Tiny, green flowers are borne in dense, 
elongated clusters on the tips of the branches, 
which are usually covered in leaves. They are 
borne in spikes or plumes and are available in a 
variety of colours including white, green, pink, 
and purplish. Seed
A shiny black coloration is common among the 
small seeds, as opposed to the cream coloration 
found among grain types. There are up to 3000 
seeds g
-1 in this variety. The tiny, lens-shaped 
seeds are usually a pale colour due to their size 
and shape. 5.7.6 Climatic requirements
Temperature
Amaranth grows well in arid conditions and is 
extremely drought tolerant.

The soil temperature 
must be between 18 and 25
oC for amaranth 
seeds to germinate, and the air temperature 
must be above 25
oC for optimal growth. When 
the temperature drops below 18oC, the growth 
stops. A major factor influencing the growth of 
Amaranth plants during the growing season is 
the number of growing days at the appropriate 
temperature during the growing season. Lower temperatures and shorter days will 
cause flowering to occur, which will result in a 
reduction in leaf production. As long as the crop grows during the summer, 
when the rains begin to fall, there should be no 
problem with frost damage. Frost, on the other 
hand, has a significant impact on the harvesting 
of the crop. The fact that amaranth is an annual 
crop means that it does not mature completely 
in regions with a short growing season. Frost is 
required in order to bring the crop's growth to 
a halt. Rainfall
Grain Amaranth is said to be drought tolerant 
when compared to most vegetables, according 
to reports. Amaranth is considered to be 
drought resistant, but the exact mechanism 
by which this is accomplished is not well 
understood. In extremely dry conditions, one 
of its characteristics that helps it is its ability 
to wilt temporarily before reviving after a 
heavy rainstorm.

Because it has a low capacity 
for water consumption, the crop is unable 
to withstand waterlogging in its early stages. When a plant is subjected to prolonged periods 
of severe drought, it flowers prematurely and 
fails to produce any leaves. 5.7.7 Soil requirements
A variety of soil types, including marginal soils, 
can be used to grow this crop, but it performs 
best in fertile, well-drained soils and deeper 
soils, according to the USDA. In order to produce 
an early and heavy yield, loose and friable soils 
with high organic matter content are ideal. It 
is possible to achieve good stands by selecting 
soils with lower clay content and managing the 
seedbed to reduce the likelihood of crusting in 
the seedbed.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
326
To ensure rapid germination and emergence, 
amaranth requires good seed-to-soil contact, 
and sufficient soil moisture must be maintained 
at the seeding depth throughout the first few 
weeks of establishment. The growth of the 
vegetable Amaranth is negatively affected by 
soil pH levels ranging between 5,5-7,5. A soil 
with a pH of 6.44 has the potential to produce 
high yields. If the plants are properly cared for, it 
should be possible to harvest leaves every two 
weeks if the plants are properly maintained. 5.7.8 Propagation
Amaranth grows naturally, but can also be 
propagated from seed. 5.7.9 Soil preparation
Fill in any gaps in the soil with cattle or chicken 
manure or compost at a rate of one bucketful 
per 1 m
2 after it has been thoroughly prepared. It is critical to have a fine, firm seedbed for 
successful seeding. Seedbed preparation can be 
accomplished with a disc or spike-tooth harrow, 
followed by cult packing and planting, which 
should be accomplished with a planter equipped 
with press wheels, if possible. Depending on the 
soil texture and surface moisture at the time of 
planting, seeds should be planted no deeper 
than 1.25 cm deep at most.

Crusting can be a 
serious problem, and although no solutions 
have been discovered, rotary hoeing may be of 
assistance in some cases. 5.7.10 Field layout and design
Although no optimal plant population has been 
identified, it is believed that approximately 272 
kg seed ha
-1 is a reasonable estimate. Amaranth 
trials have been reported to use row widths 
of 762 mm as the industry standard. The crop 
provides good shade for the ground at this row 
spacing, and the wide rows make it easy to use 
a row crop cultivator to control weeds in the 
field. This is critical because there are currently 
no registered herbicides for Amaranth. When Amaranth plants are spaced closer 
together, there appears to be an excessive 
amount of competition among them. As a 
result, plants grow shorter and less vigorously, 
and grain heads grow smaller . On the plus side, 
planting only 0.9 kg seed acre
-1, which is the 
recommended rate, results in an abundance of 
seedlings, allowing a large number to be lost 
while still having enough left over to establish a 
sufficient stand. Plants are spaced approximately 
1 m apart in rows. 5.7.11 Planting
Sow seeds in seedbeds or directly in rows in 
the field, depending on your preference.

It is 
preferable for nurseries to be located in flat 
areas with fertile soil and close to a water 
source, rather than in shaded areas. Seed and 
sand should be mixed in a one-to-two-to-one 
ratio before sowing at a depth of 0.5-1 cm in 
rows or broadcasting directly in the field. After 
covering the seeds with a thin layer of soil and 
watering them, they are ready to plant. If you 
prefer, you can water the seed first, then spread 
it over the soil and cover it with a thin layer of 
dry soil. Depending on the size of the plants, the 
optimal spacing ranges from 20 x 20 cm to 50 x 
50 cm. If you are transplanting seedlings, they 
will be ready to transplant after 3 to 5 weeks 
when they have four true leaves on their stems 
(15 cm). 5.7.12 Fertilisation
To get the best results from chemical fertiliser, 
look for a 3:1:3 or 3:12: (NPK) mixture. By 
hoeing a furrow and applying the fertiliser

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
327
mixture at a rate of 40 g m-1 in the bottom of the 
furrow, you can increase the yield significantly. A normal-sized tea cup holds approximately 
200 g of chemical fertiliser, which is sufficient to 
cover 5 m. To mix fertiliser into the soil after it 
has been evenly spread in the furrow, use a stick 
to stir the fertiliser into the soil. After you have 
watered the furrow, you can start making the 
holes for the transplants. To achieve exceptional 
growth, apply limestone ammonium nitrate 
(LAN) at a rate of 20 gm
-2 when the plants 
have approximately 5-6 leaves on them. The 
LAN should be spread along a row of 10 m, 
one teacup at a time. LAN fertiliser should be 
applied to a growing crop by opening a furrow 
with a hoe about 10-15 cm away from the row 
of plants, mixing the fertiliser into the soil with a 
stick, and then watering the furrow and closing 
it again. In the case of poultry or pig manure, apply a 
10L bucket in a band approximately 20 cm wide 
over a length of 15 m, work the manure into the 
topsoil, thoroughly water the band, and wait 
1 or 2 weeks before planting.

When applying 
kraal manure, follow the same procedure as 
described above, but apply a 10 L bucket over a 
5 m length of the land. 5.7.13 Irrigation
Plants that are suffering from drought will begin 
to flower and will cease to produce leaves 
unless they are watered regularly. The amount 
of water required by crops varies depending 
on their growth stage, soil type, and weather 
conditions (hot or cold). Because sandy soils dry 
quickly, it will be necessary to irrigate them on 
a regular basis. Clay soils, on the other hand, 
drain more slowly than sandy soils and retain 
more water than their sandy counterparts. There are a few "rules of thumb" that can be 
used as a starting point for determining the 
frequency and volume of irrigation. Drought-
prone sandy soils should be watered three 
times weekly, while sandy loam soils should be 
watered once a week. Irrigation should be done 
once a week on clay, clay loam, and loam soils. Irrigate a 1 m x 1 m plot with 4 L water day
-1 
for the first 30-35 days after transplanting for 
the best results. Following that, irrigate a 1 m 
x 1 m plot with between 5-6 L day
-1, depending 
on the weather . Amaranth can be irrigated with 
both sprinklers and drip irrigation; however, 
drip irrigation saves the most water of the two.

If you must use a sprinkler irrigation system, 
avoid watering your plants in the late afternoon 
to avoid foliar disease problems. Overwatering 
causes nutrients to be leached away from 
the roots of the plants, so careful planning of 
irrigation volume and frequency is required to 
avoid crop stress and to produce large, healthy 
vegetable Amaranth plants. 5.7.14 Weed control
Weeds are the most serious pest in the 
production of amaranth. Before planting the 
Amaranth, weeds are controlled either by tillage 
or by applying a contact herbicide before the 
Amaranth is planted. Because amaranth grows 
slowly during the first few weeks, three or four 
cultivations may be required to keep weeds 
under control during this time (no selective 
herbicides are registered for use with Amaranth). Grain Amaranth seeds do not go into dormancy, 
and their growth is not particularly vigorous in 
the early stages of the growing season. So there 
is little chance of it becoming a weed problem 
in succeeding crops after it has been harvested. However, although cover crops and no-till 
planting can help prevent weed seeds from 
germinating, Amaranth seedlings grow slowly 
and are easily overtaken by early weeds in the 
first few weeks after planting. Once Amaranth

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
328
reaches a height of 6-10 inches, it will begin to 
grow at a rapid rate, allowing it to outperform 
other emerging weeds. 5.7.15 Pest and disease control
Amaranth is susceptible to a variety of insects, 
though the plants are able to recover after being 
attacked by the majority of leaf-chewing insects 
that feed on them. Amaranth is susceptible 
to insect pests such as the tarnished plant 
bug, leaf miners, flea beetle, grasshoppers, 
caterpillars, and amaranth weevils, which are all 
potentially damaging. Flea beetles are known 
to cause damage to young leaf tissue. The adult 
amaranth weevil feeds on the leaves of the 
plant, but the larval stage is more destructive 
because it bores into the central tissue of roots 
and occasionally stems, causing rotting and 
potential lodging in the process. Disease problems can arise in large monoculture 
production systems, which is why they should 
be avoided. Phoma and Rhizoctonia can both 
cause damping-off of young seedlings in certain 
environmental conditions. Pythium can also 
cause stem canker, which is caused by Pythium 
or Rhizoctonia, in some situations. A.

tricolour 
appears to be particularly susceptible to the 
fungus Phomopsis, which colonises the leaves 
and stems of the plant and causes dieback. Pests and diseases are eliminated through 
effective weed control. Weeds have the potential 
to serve as a host for pests and diseases. Clean 
seed and the establishment of sterilised soils 
before sowing seeds can help to reduce the 
amount of damping-off that occurs. Seed trays 
should be cleaned with commercial bleach after 
each planting. Plant seedlings in well-drained 
soil in a well-ventilated area to ensure a healthy 
start. 5.7.16 Other cultivation practices
It is possible to grow amaranth as a double 
crop following wheat or canola. In order for 
amaranth to be successful, it must be planted in 
a two-year rotation with another crop. 5.7.17 Harvesting maturity 
The majority of amaranth cultivars grow quickly 
and can be harvested between 30-55 days after 
sowing, when they reach a height of 0.06 m. Amaranth cultivars are available in a variety of 
colours and sizes. The timing of harvest is not 
as straightforward as it is with commodity crops 
such as corn and soybeans. It is possible to lose the majority of the seed if 
harvesting techniques are not followed carefully.

It is necessary for a killing frost to occur before 
harvesting can begin, followed by a week of dry 
weather to allow the crop to become drier in 
preparation for harvesting. When the plants are harvested at regular 
intervals, begin picking the leaves 8 weeks after 
sowing or 4 weeks after transplanting to ensure 
that the plants are harvested regularly. It is 
possible to harvest a small amount of leaves on 
a daily basis in small quantities. When dealing 
with large quantities, it is recommended that 
you do so every two weeks. By removing flowers 
from the plant, it is possible to maintain leaf 
production.

Leaves can be harvested in two ways:
• Picking of individual leaves when these are 
the size of the palm of your hand.
• Breaking off the leaves around the terminal 
growth tips of the stems. This is done by 
pulling one hand up towards the growth tip 
and breaking off the leaves with the other 
hand.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
329
5.7.18 Seed harvesting
When the leaves of the plant begin to turn 
yellow, it is time to harvest the seeds. It is 
necessary to place the harvested seed heads 
on a clean plastic sheet and allow them to dry 
in the shade. Seeds can be easily threshed by 
hand and cleaned by the wind blowing through 
the fields. If you want to avoid insect damage, 
store properly dried seeds in a tightly sealed 
container . 5.7.19 Post-harvest handling
Thorough planning in terms of handling, 
grading, packing and storage of products should 
be done. 5.7.20 Screening
It is possible to remove the dry seeds by placing 
them in a shallow bowl and swirling them 
around until the large pieces of flowers rise to 
the top of the bowl, where they can be easily 
removed. By tipping the bowl, you can get rid of 
a lot of the chaff that has accumulated. Remove 
any remaining small particles of flowers or dirt 
from the seed by shaking it through a small 
mesh screen about the size of a window screen 
for a couple of minutes. It is also effective to 
winnow the seed in a light breeze to remove the 
flowers and chaff from the seed. Because the 
seeds are so light, it is critical to winnow them 
carefully in only a light breeze.

5.7.21 Grading
A gravity table can be used to separate particles 
that are the same size but have different 
weights, such as dark pigweed seeds, by using 
the force of gravity. 5.7.22 Packing
Immediately following harvest, the leaves are 
placed in a bag and sold on the same day of 
harvest to avoid quality degradation. The leaves, 
on the other hand, can be stored in containers 
in places where there is cooling storage. 5.7.23 Storage
The maximum amount of moisture that can be 
stored in the grain is approximately 11 percent. By blowing air across the Amaranth, you can dry 
small quantities of grain quickly and efficiently. The heated air may be required at certain times. Ideally, grain should be stored in wooden storage 
bins or heavy-duty (4 or 5-ply) paper bags after 
it has been cleaned and dried to prevent mould 
growth. It is critical to store properly dried seeds 
in a tightly sealed container in order to prevent 
contamination. 5.7.24 Preserving methods
Washing the leaves and drying them in the 
shade allows you to store them for up to a year, 
ready to use during the winter months. Cooked 
leaves can be dried and stored for later use. Fresh leaves can be stored in the refrigerator for 
up to two weeks.

5.7.25 Transport
Amaranth requires refrigerated transport to 
retain the turgidity of the leaves. 5.7.26 Marketing
Both the domestic and international markets are 
overrun with exotic crops, making it difficult to 
introduce new indigenous varieties. As a result, 
indigenous crops such as amaranth continue to 
be primarily the crops of small farmers, and they 
are primarily consumed in the regions where

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
330
they are grown. The leaves of this crop are sold 
by street hawkers in Mpumalanga, KwaZulu-
Natal, and the Eastern Cape, among other 
places. Farmers who grow amaranth have sold 
their crop in a variety of ways over the years. Some companies sell small bags of whole-grain 
or flour to consumers who order it through the 
mail. Many of these buyers are allergic to wheat 
products, which is understandable. Other 
growers sell their produce to the local market, 
as well as to health food stores and restaurants 
in the area. Also present are some middlemen 
who purchase grain from farmers and sell it 
to the larger health-food corporations on a 
commission basis. As has been the case with leafy Amaranth, the 
grain market continues to be the most difficult 
obstacle to the development of this crop. Even 
though Amaranth is beneficial to commercial 
farmers, the relatively high price of the grain 
is a factor in limiting the extent to which it 
is currently used in the food marketplace. However, the beneficial characteristics of 
amaranth grain, as well as its adaptability to a 
wide range of growing environments, make it a 
very promising crop for the future of agriculture.

Despite the fact that the market for amaranth is 
small, the popularity of grain as food is growing 
as a result of its nutritional benefits. Despite the fact that the physical characteristics 
of grain starch have the potential to be valuable 
for both food and industrial applications, no 
products based on these characteristics have 
been commercialised to date. When undertaking such initiatives, marketing 
considerations must be taken into account in 
order to ensure that the communities reap 
the greatest possible benefit from the efforts 
they have made to re-establish and propagate 
underutilised, but undeniably popular species. A farmer who enters the market with grain 
harvested from several hundred hectares 
of Amaranth could cause a glut of grain and 
consequently lower prices by a significant 
margin. This is why amaranth should only be 
planted after a market for the crop has been 
identified, and preferably after a contract with a 
buyer has been drafted with the buyer . 5.7.27 Utilization
Leaf Amaranth is a steamed vegetable that can 
be added to soups and stews. One of the reasons 
there has been a recent resurgence of interest in 
amaranth is the nutritional benefits it provides.

The grain contains a small amount of protein 
(12-17%) and is high in lysine, an amino acid 
that is found in small amounts in other grains. As a result of the grain's high fibre content and 
low saturated fat content, it is popular in the 
health food market. Calcium, iron, and vitamin 
C are among the nutrients found in abundance 
in this food. Potassium, vitamin A, riboflavin, 
and niacin are also abundant, and protein is 
above-average in this food. Humans have used grain amaranth for food in 
a variety of ways for thousands of years. The 
ground grain is used in a variety of products such 
as breads, noodles, pancakes, cereals, granola, 
cookies, and other flour-based items. The grain 
can be prepared in a variety of ways, including 
popping it like popcorn or flaking it like oatmeal. Currently, there are more than 40 products on 
the market that contain Amaranth extract. There is little information available about the 
production and use of amaranth as a forage 
crop. The leaves, stem, and head of the plant 
are said to be high in protein (15-24% on a dry-
matter basis). Redroot pigweed, a relative of 
grain Amaranth, has been shown to contain 
24% crude protein and 79% in vitro digestible 
dry matter, according to research.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
331
Plants like vegetable amaranths, which are 
closely related, produce 30-60 tonnes of 
silage per hectare of land (80% moisture). Depending on the results of further research, 
grain Amaranth may prove to be a viable silage 
alternative in areas where maize silage yields 
are low due to moisture limitations. There are many different species of Amaranth 
that are grown for food. The edible seeds of 
some types of Amaranth are grown for human 
consumption, while the edible greens of others 
are grown for human consumption. It is widely 
cultivated in West Africa for its edible greens, 
particularly in Sierra Leone, and is one of the 
most important crops in the region. The plant 
is a fast-growing annual that thrives in high 
temperatures, allowing it to reach its maximum 
height of 5-6 feet in height. 5.8 COWPEAS
5.8.1 Introduction
Various studies in South Africa have emphasized 
the importance of indigenous food crops in 
meeting many of the basic needs of rural 
communities, including serving as dietary 
supplements, animal fodder, and generating 
income. Cowpea has been identified as a significant 
indigenous food crop in South Africa, with a 
wide range of applications and advantages 
(Figure 27).

Seeds can be consumed fresh or 
dried for storage, leaves can be consumed as 
a vegetable, and plants can be used as green 
manure. It can be used as grazing for livestock 
or baled for use as hay or silage for them. Figure 27 Cowpea (Vigna unguiculata). Source: ARC.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
332
Cowpea is generally regarded as a drought-
tolerant crop that is also simple to grow. It is 
an especially good crop for rural areas, where 
the rural poor typically rely on an energy-dense 
carbohydrate food as their staple food, to which 
a protein-dense food such as cowpea can be 
added to provide a more balanced diet. The 
protein content ranges between 24-36%, with 
lysine being the most abundant amino acid 
found. Besides being beneficial for fixing atmospheric 
nitrogen, it also helps to increase soil organic 
matter content while improving soil structure. The crop can be grown in rotation with a variety 
of other crops such as vegetables, herbs, cut 
flowers, annual ornamentals, and root crops, 
among others. 5.8.2 Soil requirements
Cowpeas can be grown in a wide variety of soil 
types, ranging from heavy clays to sandy soils, 
provided that the soils are well-drained. It is 
unable to withstand being submerged in water 
or being flooded. It is preferable to have medium 
loam soil with a clay content ranging between 
15-35%. A pH range of 5.5-6.5 is preferred for 
soil acidity.

5.8.3 Climatic requirements
Cowpea is primarily a hot-weather crop that 
grows well in semi-arid climates, according to 
the USDA. Temperatures in excess of 21
oC are 
favorable for vegetative growth. Temperatures 
above 35
oC can cause flower and pod shedding, 
which can reduce yields. Cowpeas are sensitive 
to frost and can suffer damage if they are 
exposed to temperatures ranging from 5-10
oC 
for periods as short as 24 hours. It is also sensitive 
to day length, but cultivars with short days, day 
neutral days, and long days are available. 5.8.4 Cultivars
There are primarily three types of plants 
classified according to their growth habits: 
upright, semi-upright, and runner . Because 
runner and semi-runner cultivars have a 
tendency to produce fruit that ripens over a 
longer period of time, they are more suitable for 
use as a vegetable, for forage, for silage, and as 
green manure. Consult with your seed company 
for assistance in selecting a cultivar . 5.8.5 Crop rotation
Cowpeas have long been recognized for their 
value in crop rotation. The growth of grain crops 
and other vegetables on land that had been 
planted with cowpeas the previous season is 
noticeably improved.

This is due to the residual 
effect of nitrogen (N) bound by the cowpeas 
from the previous season, which has caused the 
crop to fail. It is important, however, to avoid 
other leguminous crops in a rotation system 
because they are more or less susceptible to 
the same pests and diseases as cowpeas and 
should be avoided if possible. 5.8.6 Soil preparation
The majority of cowpeas are grown by gardeners 
and small-scale farmers in South Africa using 
traditional agricultural methods and hand tools, 
frequently in intercropping systems with maize, 
sorghum, and millet. It is critical to remove all 
weeds and loosen the soil deeply with a fork 
before applying fertiliser if necessary. When 
applying fertiliser, work the fertiliser into the 
soil with the fork, breaking up clods and evening 
out the soil. The final seedbed must be uniform, 
fine, slightly firmed, free of clods, and free of 
weeds and weed remnants. It should also be 
free of weeds and weed remains.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
333
5.8.7 Planting time
Planting too soon may result in poor 
germination. Planting too late may result in crop 
losses if a frost occurs too soon, or it may not be 
possible for the pods to form before the frost 
arrives. Cowpeas are planted after the first rains 
in October and continue to grow until the end 
of December . The optimal planting date varies 
depending on the cultivar and the geographical 
location. Obtain information from your local 
extension services or seed supplier about the 
best time to plant in your specific area and 
cultivar choice. 5.8.8 Growth period
It is influenced by a variety of factors, including 
the cultivar, planting date, soil type, rainfall, and 
temperature over the course of the season. The 
following are general guidelines for growing 
cowpeas during their growth period (see Table 
10). 5.8.9 Seed
It is critical that you purchase high-quality seed 
from your cooperative or seed supplier . The 
importance of selecting healthy plants when 
growing your own seed cannot be overstated 
when growing your own seed. Some diseases 
caused by viruses and bacteria are frequently 
transmitted through seed.

5.8.10 Spacing
Types that stand upright perform better in 
narrower rows with 0.9 m between rows and 
10 cm between plants (12 kg seed ha
-1). When 
there is enough water available (through heavy 
rainfall or irrigation), the spacing between 
plants can be reduced to 0.45 m apart and 10 
cm between plants (24 kg of seed per ha). Semi-runners and runners do better in wider 
rows that are 1.5 m apart and 10 cm between 
plants, as opposed to narrower rows (9 kg seed 
ha
-1). 5.8.11 Fertilisation
Cowpeas grow best in heavier clay soils with 
high organic content, so fertilizing them is 
rarely necessary. On sandy soils, approximately 
100 gm
-2 of a 3:2:1 (28) fertiliser mixture should 
be sufficient to produce a good crop. It is 
preferable, however, to have the soil tested and 
then consult with your available expertise to 
develop a fertilizing programme for you based 
on the results of the soil test. Table 10 Growth comparisons for cowpeas. Growth stage Cowpea types Growth period 
(days)
Planting to 50% flowering
Upright 50
Runner 60
Planting to green uses (hay, vegetable, green manure, etc.)
Upright 100
Runner 120
Planting to harvesting of grain
Upright 120
Runner 140

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
334
5.8.12 Water requirements
However, even though it is generally considered 
to be a drought-tolerant crop, moisture stress 
during the period from emergence to the 
development of the first flower can cause a 
significant reduction in seed yield. The use of 
three or four irrigations (at 25 mm per irrigation) 
during this period (only to supplement rainfall if 
necessary) is recommended if water is readily 
available. 5.8.13 Planting process
In all cases, cowpeas should be planted at a 
depth of 4 cm or deeper . In a vegetable garden, 
the number of rows required for planting can 
be determined, and then a furrow 4 cm deep 
with a spade or hand hoe can be dug, the seeds 
placed 10 cm apart in the furrows, and the soil 
covered with 4 cm of soil can be completed. Fertilisers should not be allowed to come into 
contact with seedlings. 5.8.14 Cultivation after planting
No extra cultivation is needed, except for weed 
control. 5.8.15 Weed control
Herbicides can be used to control weeds, as 
can manual or mechanical removal, as well as 
herbicide application. The weeds are typically 
pulled out by hand or with a hand hoe by the 
average home gardener .

Herbicides are not 
recommended for use in vegetable gardens 
because they are expensive and can cause 
damage to other crops as well as vegetables. 5.8.16 Diseases
Cowpeas can be infected by virus, bacterial and 
fungal diseases. Cowpea mosaic virus: Chlorotic lesions on 
leaves which may develop reddish brown 
necrosis. Control: Avoid practices that injure plants, 
sanitation is important and use good healthy 
seed. Bacterial canker: Swollen canker-like lesions 
on the stem. Leaves, stems, pods and seed are 
prone to the disease. Control: Use only good healthy seed. Damping-off (Fungus): Cause pre-and post-
emergence wilting and dying of seedlings. The 
disease is usually only important in humid, 
warm, tropical conditions. Control: The disease is difficult to control. Avoid 
infestation by good rotational practices. Fusarium wilt (Fungus): The vascular tissue of 
the plant becomes reddish extending to the 
tips of the runners. Leaves turn yellow in color 
and drop, followed by fairly rapid wilting of the 
whole plant. Control: Resistant cultivars and crop rotation. Anthracnose (Fungus): Most legumes affected. Affect all above-ground plant parts, especially 
the stems, peduncles and petioles. Control: Use healthy seed and practice good 
crop rotation.

Other diseases: Other diseases that can be a 
problem are stem rot, leaf spot, downy mildew, 
stem canker and various virus diseases. Control: The most effective and cheapest 
control measure are crop rotation and planting 
only healthy seed and resistant cultivars if 
available.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
335
5.8.17 Harvesting
Fresh, immature seed pods and seeds are 
boiled and eaten as a vegetable in place of the 
mature seeds. It is possible to grow cowpea as 
a dual-purpose crop, in which the green pods 
are harvested for use as a vegetable and the 
remaining plant material is used for animal feed. Seeds that are not yet mature can be roasted 
and eaten as a snack in the same manner as 
groundnuts. Shoots, leaves, and growth points 
from young plants are harvested, boiled, and 
consumed as spinach. When fresh vegetables are in short supply, 
mature leaves can be boiled, drained, and 
then dried in the sun before being stored and 
later used as a meat substitute or as a relish to 
supplement the diet. In some cases, the preparation of cowpeas in 
its various forms is regarded as indigenous 
knowledge that belongs to the communities 
who practice it. The ARC-VOP can provide you 
with additional information in this regard. 5.9 PIGEON PEAS
Scientific name: Cajanus cajan L.

Common names: Pigeon peas; Letlhodi; Ndozi; 
Duifert; Udali; Dithlodi; Tintoji
5.9.1 Background
Pigeon peas are believed to have originated in 
India and first appeared in West Africa around 
2000 BC, which is considered to be their second 
major centre of origin (Figure 28 ). Pigeon peas 
are currently found in abundance throughout 
the tropics and subtropics of the world. They 
are grown in India, Malaysia, Indonesia, the 
Philippines, the Caribbean, and East and West 
Africa, among other countries. 5.9.2 Production areas
Limpopo-Bohlabela, Capricorn and Mopani 
Districts. Mpumalanga - Gert Sibande, Ekangala, 
Ehlanzeni South and North districts. Figure 28 Pigeon pea (Cajanus cajan). Source: Planting Man.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
336
5.9.3 Climatic requirements
Pigeon peas thrive in temperatures ranging 
from 18-35oC. For the best possible yield, bright 
sunlight is required. Waterlogging and frost 
damage the plants, making them vulnerable. 5.9.4 Soil requirements
Growing conditions for the crop are excellent in 
a wide range of soil types, ranging from sandy to 
heavy loams, with well-drained medium heavy 
loams being the best. In order for it to work, it needs a pH level ranging 
from 5-7. Excessive acidity causes nodulation 
to be inhibited, and the plants become chlorotic 
and susceptible to die-back disease. 5.9.5 Rainfall
Pigeon peas are a legume crop that is drought 
tolerant, but they are not frosted tolerant. These plants are also tolerant of a wide range 
of rainfall amounts; however, optimal rainfall 
amounts ranging between 600-1000 mm year
-
1 are preferred over anything else. Pigeon peas 
produce a large number of seeds in dry areas 
with less than 600 mm of annual rainfall because 
the crop matures early and the incidence of 
pest damage is low. Pigeon peas are particularly 
successful in dry areas with less than 600mm 
of annual rainfall.

It is best for pigeon peas to 
grow in moist conditions during their first two 
growing seasons and in dry conditions during 
their flowering and harvesting stages. 5.9.6 Propagation material
Pigeon peas are best established by direct 
seeding in a well-prepared seedbed, rather than 
by transplanting from another location. The use 
of a cowpea group strain of Rhizobium is not 
usually required, but it can be done if desired. 5.9.7 Soil preparation
Pigeon peas do best in a seedbed that has been 
prepared by deep tillage to reduce the amount 
of weeds. Primary tillage in the autumn allows 
the soil to dry and warm up more quickly in the 
spring, allowing for earlier seeding in the spring. 5.9.8 Planting
Planting takes place between October and 
December, with the seeds being planted 
approximately 4-5 cm deep. It is possible to plant 
seeds with a seed rate ranging from 45-67 kg 
ha
-1, or to seed with a maize planter, depending 
on the weather conditions. Approximately 70-
90 cm between the long and medium duration 
plants is the recommended interspacing. 5.9.9 Fertilisation
Plants, in general, do not respond well to nitrogen 
and phosphorus fertilisers, and they require 
adequate amounts of calcium, potassium, 
and magnesium.

In order to increase nitrogen 
fixation in such plants, Rhizobium must be 
introduced into the plant. Plants such as pigeon 
peas have the ability to survive and produce high 
economic returns in arid climates and low-input 
farming systems. The root nodules of the peas 
enrich the soil by supplying approximately 40 kg 
N ha
-1 of land planted with them. Pigeon peas 
can be used as a green manure crop because of 
their high nitrogen content. 5.9.10 Irrigation
If you live in a dry area with less than 400 mm 
of annual rainfall per year, you can supplement 
your water supply with irrigation for the first 
two growing months. Irrigation should be 
discontinued between the time of flowering and 
harvesting in order to reduce damage caused 
by pests and diseases.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
337
5.9.11 Weed control
As a result of their slow initial growth rate, 
pigeon peas are extremely sensitive to weed 
competition, particularly during their first 45-60 
days of growth. Effective weed control during the crop's early 
growth stages is one of the most important 
factors in achieving high yields, particularly 
during the first 4-8 weeks of growth. Following 
that, herbicides such as Alachlor and 
Metolachlor should be applied at a rate of 1.25 
L ha
-1 within 2-3 days of planting, according to 
the manufacturer's instructions. 5.9.12 Pest and disease control
Pests and diseases such as pod-sucking bugs 
(Clavigralla spp. ), pod fly (Melanagromyza 
spp.), pod borers (Helicoverpa armigera ), as 
well as rust, downy mildew, and Cercospora leaf 
spot, have a negative impact on the productivity 
of pigeon peas and result in poor-quality seed 
production. Crop stand is reduced by pests and 
diseases; however, pest-resistant cultivars, crop 
rotation, weed removal and inoculation with 
a cowpea group strain of Rhizobium as well 
as intercropping with cereals can be used to 
control these problems.

5.9.13 Harvesting methods
The majority of the seed pods are harvested 
by hand, though mechanical harvesting can 
also be used in some cases. A sickle is used to 
harvest the mature crop, which is harvested by 
cutting the entire plant in half. Harvest the dry 
grain when 80% of the pods are fully matured 
and have turned brown if you want to make 
flour . When a second ratoon crop is required, 
the pods can be harvested by hand when they 
reach maturity. 5.9.14 Uses
Human uses: Pigeon peas are primarily grown 
as a vegetable food crop, where they provide 
a good source of protein, carbohydrates, 
and other minerals, among other things. The 
flavourful seeds are consumed in a variety of 
forms, including fresh, shelled, and dried. For 
cooking purposes, mature seeds are soaked for 
several hours, pounded, and fried or steam-
cooked in Africa and Indonesia. Local South 
Africans in Mpumalanga use the whole seed to 
make porridge and soup, either with or without 
meat, according to their tradition. Animal consumption : Pigeon peas are an 
excellent form of fodder that can be used for 
animal and poultry feed. Other uses: Pigeon peas are also important 
for nitrogen fixation and are a good crop for 
intercropping and crop rotation, according to 
the USDA.

The branches and stems can be used 
to make baskets and fuel, among other things. It can also be used as a shadow crop in certain 
situations. 5.10 BAMBARA GROUND NUTS 
(VIGNA SUBTERRANEA) 
Classification
Scientific name: Vigna subterranea (L.) Verdc
Common names: Bambara groundnut; 
Jugoboon, ditloo-marapo, izindlubu, Indlubu, 
jugo bean, nduhu, phonda, ndlowu, njugo, 
tindlubu, inhlowa
5.10.1 Origin and distribution
Bambara groundnut, also known as Nyimo in 
Zimbabwe and Izindlubu in South Africa, is a 
type of groundnut that originated in North Africa 
and was brought to South Africa by indigenous

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
338
people (Figure 29). It is a biennial legume with 
a well-developed tap root system that grows 
in the ground. Women have traditionally been 
the primary producers of bambara, which is 
grown in semi-arid regions where water is 
scarce and where they lack access to inorganic 
fertilisers as well as little guidance on improved 
production practices. Bambara is considered 
to be a significant source of protein in these 
communities. Its protein content is comparable 
to, and in some cases superior to, that of other 
well-established legumes, making it an excellent 
complement to diets based on cereal grains. As a legume, it also has the ability to replenish 
nitrogen in the soil through nitrogen fixation, 
which may be of particular importance to 
farmers who have limited access to resources. Similarly to other land races, the colours of 
bambara seeds are diverse, with the most 
common being cream, brown and red, with 
mottled and white being the most common as 
well. Because of the expansion of groundnut 
production, bambara has been relegated to the 
status of an underutilised crop in most parts 
of Africa as a result of groundnut production 
expansion.

5.10.2 Production levels in South Africa
In South Africa, it is primarily produced by local 
people for subsistence purposes, according to 
the government. It has only been in recent years 
that people have begun to sell it at farmers' 
markets (e.g. boiled groundnuts). It is grown 
as an intercrop with other crops such as maize, 
cowpeas, and melons, as well as a stand-alone 
crop. The size of bambara plots varies from 300 
to 2500 m
2 per farmer, depending on the region. Because the crop is not grown commercially in 
South Africa, and because it has been neglected 
by National Research Institutes in the past, little 
is known about the best agronomic practices 
for this crop in the country. 5.10.3 Major production areas in South Africa
The provinces of Limpopo, Mpumalanga, the 
North West, Gauteng, and KwaZulu-Natal are 
the primary production areas for bambara 
groundnut in South Africa, with the rest of 
the country following. The Mopani, Capricorn, 
Waterberg, and Vhembe Districts in the 
Limpopo Province are the primary producers of 
bambara groundnut. Figure 29 Bambara groundnut (Vigna subterranean). Source: ARC.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
339
The Ehlanzeni District in Mpumalanga is home to 
a significant amount of agricultural production. Greytown, Msinga, Nkandla, Nguthuthu, 
Makhati, and Kosibaai are among the towns 
where it is grown in KwaZulu-Natal. Smaller 
quantities are also produced in the Umzinyathi 
and Illembe Districts, respectively. 5.10.4 Varieties and cultivars
The seven types of the bambara groundnut 
cultivated varieties include:
• Black: Early maturing, usually small to 
medium-sized kernels. Mainly one-seeded. • Red: Late maturing. Kernels are large. A 
good yielder, however, it is prone to rotting. • Cream/black eye: A large kernel and a good 
yielder . • Cream/brown eye: A medium-sized kernel 
and a good yielder . • Cream / no eye: Very small pods and 
kernels. It mainly produces one seed and 
yields are lower . • Speckled/flecked/spotted: Purple color 
predominates. Kernels are small and pods 
are mainly one-seeded. • Brown: Continuous variation between light 
and dark brown. Kernels are of medium to 
large. The bambara varieties, in general, have 
photoperiod requirements that are specific to 
their location; as a result, high yielding varieties 
from one location may fail to thrive in another 
location.

As a result, rather than focusing 
on the introduction of exotic varieties, the 
emphasis has been placed on the selection and 
improvement of local cultivars. 5.10.5 Mature plant
The bambara groundnuts are herbaceous 
annual plants with creeping stems that grow 
from the ground up to the surface of the soil. It 
takes between three and six months for a plant 
to reach maturity, depending on the weather 
and the cultivar . Roots: A compact well-developed taproot with 
many profuse geotropic short lateral roots 20 
cm long. The roots form nodules for nitrogen 
fixation, in association with African wild-type 
Rhizobia. Stem: It has lateral stems, which develop from 
the root. Leaves: The leaves are trifoliate (± 5 cm long) 
and are attached to the stem by the petiole. The 
petioles are about 15 cm long, stiff and grooved, 
and the base is green or purple in colour . Leaves 
and flower buds arise alternately at each node. Leaves are pinnately trifoliate, with erect 
petioles and thickened at the base. Two stipels 
are subtended to the terminal leaflet, while 
only one is assigned to each of the two lateral 
leaflets. The terminal leaflet is larger than the 
lateral leaflets, with an average length of 6 cm 
and an average width of 3 cm.

Flowers: The flowers are typically papilionaceous 
and are borne in a raceme on long, hairy 
peduncles, which arise from the nodes on the 
stem. The branching types are usually self-
pollinated, while the spreading types are cross-
pollinated by ants. Flowering starts 30-35 days 
after sowing and may continue until the end 
of the plant’s life. After fertilisation, the flower 
stem elongates. The sepal enlarges and the fruit 
develops above or just below the soil surface. Pods: The pods usually develop underground 
after pollination and fertilisation. The unripe pod 
is yellowish-green, with up to six pods, while the 
mature pods may be yellowish-green or purple. The pod is small, about 1 to 5 cm long, round or 
slightly oval-shaped, and wrinkled with mostly 
one or sometimes two seeds. Mature pods are 
indehiscent, wrinkled, ranging from yellowish to 
reddish dark brown in colour .

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
340
Seeds: The seed is hard, smooth, usually round 
and varying in size, and can be up to 1.5 cm in 
diameter . It also varies in colour from white, 
cream, dark brown, red, or black and maybe 
speckled or patterned with a combination of 
the colours. The average seed mass is about 
500-750 g. Essential part: The essential parts are the pods. 5.10.6 Climatic and environmental 
requirements
Temperature: A fast-growing plant that 
requires warm temperatures and will not 
tolerate freezing temperatures during the 
growing season, the bambara groundnut is 
a good choice for a food source. For the crop 
to mature, it will require a growth period of 
between 110-150 days. Temperatures between 
30-35°C are ideal for the germination of the 
bambara groundnut, and sprouting can take 
anywhere from 5-21 days. The ideal daytime 
temperature for crop development is in the 
range of 20-28°C on average during the growing 
season. Extreme temperatures cause the leaves 
to die, resulting in a reduction in the amount of 
biomass produced. Rainfall: From the time of sowing until the time 
of flowering, the bambara groundnut requires 
moderate rainfall.

During the growing season, 
annual rainfall of 500 - 1200 mm is required 
per hectare. Heavy rainfall is tolerated by the 
plant, but an excessive amount of rainfall during 
harvest may result in yield losses. Additionally, 
the bambara groundnut can survive in hot, dry 
climates where other crops would fail. 5.10.7 Soil requirements
Because it can be grown in poor, sandy to 
sandy loam soils with good drainage, Bambara 
groundnut is easier to harvest than other 
groundnut varieties. The pH of the soil should 
be between 5-6.5 for Bambara groundnut to 
thrive. 5.10.8 Cultivation practices
Propagation: The bambara groundnut is 
propagated by seed. Soil preparation: The bambara groundnut 
produces the highest yields when planted in a 
field that has been deeply ploughed and has a 
fine seedbed. Ploughing followed by harrowing 
of the soil twice is recommended for compacted 
soil and weed-infested areas in order to ensure 
good germination and stand development. It is 
best to plant on a level seedbed; however, when 
conditions are extremely wet, it can be planted 
on ridges. Fertilisation: Nitrogen fertilisation in excess 
promotes vegetative growth at the expense of 
crop yield.

Potassium has no value because of a 
well-developed tap-root system that is shallowly 
located in the soil. Phosphorus fertiliser, on the 
other hand, has no beneficial effect on crop 
yield. Irrigation: Bambara groundnut is commonly 
grown under rain-fed conditions, so no statistical 
information on water management for the crop 
is available at this time. 5.10.9 Weed control
Weed control can be done chemically before 
planting, mechanically, or by hand, depending 
on the situation. Pre-emergent herbicides that 
have been approved for use on bambara fields 
can be used to control weeds in an effective 
manner . The use of hand hoes is also acceptable, 
but caution should be exercised when weeding 
around the plant, particularly during flowering, 
as the flower stalks are delicate and may break if 
handled roughly. Hoeing should be done twice 
a week to ensure that the plot is free of weeds.

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
341
5.10.10 Pest and disease control
Pest control
Root-knot nematodes have the potential to 
significantly reduce crop yields. Rodents, 
termites, ants, and cutworms are among the 
pests that can be attracted to seeds. Insects 
may attack the plant and cause it to die. Insect 
pest control measures include the use of 
insecticides, such as Malathion against aphids, 
among other things. Beetles and maize weevils 
are two of the most common storage pests. Infestation frequently begins in the field during 
seed ripening and is then transported to the 
store by the customer. Apply ash to stored seeds 
to keep them safe. Chemical products such as 
Malathion and Carbamyl are also used. Root parasite Alectra vogelii (Mhlilwane) has 
been discovered to be a serious root parasite 
of bambara, particularly in Mpumalanga, and 
has been shown to cause yield reductions of 
up to 49%. The Lowveld Research Unit has 
developed two local variety selections, MPB51 
and MPB31, that have higher yields than their 
parent varieties. Pests such as Callosobruchus maculate, 
Callosobruchus subinnotatus , and 
Callosobruchus tenocampa hilda cause problems 
during storage.

If the seeds have been shelled, 
they must be treated with insecticidal dust 
before being stored. It is sometimes necessary 
to store seeds in sand or treat them with wood 
ash in order to prevent insect damage. Disease control
Cercospora leaf spot, powdery mildew, and 
Fusarium wilt are just a few of the serious 
diseases that can affect your plants. Cercospora leaf spot is characterised by reddish-
brown circular spots on the leaves, as well as 
lesions on the stems, petioles, and pods of 
the affected plants. When the attack is severe, 
the leaves fall off and the plant may die. A 
reduction in disease incidence can be achieved 
by practising crop rotation and burning of crop 
debris from the previous season, as well as by 
using disease-resistant cultivars. Fusarium wilt is characterised by vascular 
discolouration, yellowing, necrosis, and wilting 
of the leaves. Plants that are affected become 
stunted and eventually die. Fusarium wilt can 
be controlled through the use of resistant 
cultivars and crop rotation, two practises that 
are recommended. In South Africa, there are no chemicals that 
have been approved for the control of diseases 
and pests on the bambara groundnut tree.

5.10.11 Other cultivation practices
Earthing up (Ridging)
It is necessary to earth up around the base of 
the bambara groundnut plant in order for it 
to grow properly. It has been discovered that 
earthing up after the development of flowers 
has a positive effect on yield and also makes 
harvesting more convenient. The occurrence of 
Sclerotium rolfsii can be increased by earthing 
up the area where it is found. Crop rotation
In commercial production systems, the crop 
is typically planted after the maize crop. It can 
also be grown as a stand-alone crop, but it is 
more commonly intercropped with sorghum, 
maize, and tuberous crops. 5.10.12 Harvesting
Harvest maturity
For the crop to reach its full potential, it requires a 
growth period of 110 to 150 days. Ideally, plants

Climate-Smart Agriculture _ Training Manual
Vegetable production
342
should be harvested when they begin to yellow 
or wilt, or when approximately 80% of the pods 
have reached maturity. Generally, seeds are 
considered mature when the parenchymatous 
layer surrounding the embryo has vanished and 
brown patches appear on the outer surface of 
the pod. When growing in humid and moist 
conditions, it is likely that a delay in harvesting 
will result in pod rot. Harvesting methods
Hand lifting and pulling out the plant is the 
most common method of harvesting bambara 
groundnut, but cutting the taproot with a 
groundnut harvester, ploughing out, or hoeing 
out the groundnut plant is also common. Once 
the nuts have been removed from the plant, 
they are dried and either stored or consumed 
raw. Harvesting small plots of land is often done over 
a period of several weeks or months. In some 
cases, up to half of the pods can remain in the 
soil and must be collected by hand, making 
it necessary to collect the pods in a manual 
manner . In order to minimise pod loss, plants 
should be handled gently. 5.10.13 Post-harvest handling
Sorting
Normally, seeds are separated into groups 
based on colour and size.

The small seeds are 
consumed at the household level, whereas 
the larger seeds are available for sale in the 
marketplace. Packaging
Immediately after the plants have been cut, they 
are allowed to dry for a day or two before being 
stacked in wind rows to dry further . When the 
pods rattle when the crop is shaken, it indicates 
that it is ready for shelling. After drying, the 
pods are shelled by hand with a flat stone or 
brick to create a smooth surface. To handle the 
crop on a large scale, groundnut shellers can be 
modified to work with it. Storage
Shells are commonly used to store the crop. When nuts are needed for rations, sale, or 
seed, shelling should be done as soon as they 
are harvested. As a result of the seed's high 
susceptibility to weevil damage after shelling, 
Bambara groundnuts store exceptionally well in 
their pods. 5.10.14 Marketing
The sale of bambara groundnuts in local 
markets, alongside other foods such as 
boiled groundnuts, has only recently become 
commonplace. In Bambara, the majority of 
the crop is grown by local people, primarily for 
their own consumption, with any surplus being 
sold by individuals who engage in street sales 
at local markets.

When it comes to marketing 
the bambara groundnut, the size and colour 
of the seeds are important considerations. The bambara groundnut has not yet reached 
a significant level of value on the international 
market. 5.10.15 Utilization
• Bambara groundnuts are essentially grown 
for human consumption
• It can be eaten fresh or grilled while still 
immature
• Seeds are pounded into flour and used to 
make porridge with maize
• Leaves can be used for animal feed after 
harvesting

What are fruit vegetables? Vegetable-like fruit types are vegetables formed from the fruit of the plants 
that bear them. Major production areas in South Africa
Fruit vegetables are produced in all the South African provinces. Limpopo 
Province is the major production area both in the Northern Lowveld and 
far northern areas of Limpopo. The other main producing areas are the 
Onderberg area of Mpumalanga Province and Border area of the East-
ern Cape Province. Production is very limited in the winter months and 
tomatoes can only be produced in frost-free areas during winter, or under 
protection like tunnels. Climatic requirements
This annual plant is classi ﬁ ed as a warm-season crop. Low tempera-
tures, even a few degrees above freezing, may cause serious damage 
to both the plant and fruit. The optimum temperature for growth, yield 
and fruit quality of tomatoes is an average daily mean of 20°C to 24°C. At temperatures below 12°C or above 35°C, ﬂ owers are often shed, with 
a consequent poor fruit set; the quality of the fruit produced under such 
conditions may also be detrimentally affected. Even in frost-free areas, 
winter production of tomatoes should not be attempted where mean tem-
peratures frequently drop below the critical margin of 12°C.

The best soil 
temperature for germination of tomato seed is from 15°C to 30°C. At such 
temperatures plant emergence occurs in about 7 to 10 days. At tempera-
tures of only 10°C this period may extend to three weeks or longer, and 
emergence tends to be more uneven and poorer. The germination capac-
ity is detrimentally affected at soil temperatures of above 35°C. Hot, dry 
winds may cause an excessive ﬂ ower drop, even when soils are moist. Strong winds can cause serious physical damage. Continuous moist, 
rainy weather promotes the occurrence and spread of leaf diseases and 
makes their control more dif ﬁ cult. For best results, tomatoes should be 
grown in relatively dry areas under irrigation. Soil requirements
Tomatoes should ideally be grown in deep, fertile, humus-rich, free-drain-
ing, but moisture retentive soils, which are free of nematodes. Sandy 
loam to clay loam soils, with a clay content of between 15 and 35%, are 
considered to be the most suitable. Sandy or gravelly soils are accept-
able, provided the soil moisture content can be kept at the desired high level.

Heavy clay soils are less suitable because the slower drainage can cause unfavourable 
waterlogging during periods of prolonged or heavy rains, particularly where these occur just after a good irrigation. In entirely unrestricted soils, a few tomato roots may 
penetrate to a depth of over 2 m, but the greatest concentration of roots occurs in the top 600 mm of soil, which is considered to be the effective rooting depth of this 
plant. Soils with a minimum depth of 600 mm should therefore be selected, with even deeper soils receiving preference. The tomato does well in humus-rich soils and 
will respond well if grown after a green manure or soil-improving crop. Weed management
Weeds present problems either because they compete for light, water and nutrients, or because they transmit diseases and pests. Therefore, controlling weed growth 
is very important. It is best to remove weeds before they produce seeds. Weeds can be removed by pulling them out by hand or by hoeing just under the surface of 
the soil. To avoid damaging the roots, do not weed by hoe too often on too deep. Weed growth can be suppressed or slowed down by covering the ground with mulch. Mulch consists of plant remains such as rice straw, sugar-cane pulp (bagasse), grass and/or leaves.

Be careful not to use weeds with seeds in the mulch. Also do not 
to use plant remains from the Solanaceae family, as these can transmit pests and diseases. Potential problems and management
Generally, peppers are problem free. The same pests and diseases that plague other members of the nightshade family (tomatoes, potatoes, and eggplants), however, 
will occasionally attack peppers. With a few precautions, you can keep your peppers “clean.” Use organic pesticides to eliminate common pests. Destructive caterpillars 
like cutworms, tomato hornworms, and borers are easily controlled with Bacillus thuringiensis (BT or Thuricide). Rotenone and pyrethrum will readily handle pepper 
maggots and weevils, leafminers, ﬂ ea beetles, and aphids. Plant disease-resistant pepper varieties, especially if anthracnose, mosaic, and bacterial spot are problems 
in your area. (Ask veteran gardeners in your neighbourhood or t he local or district Extension Ofﬁ cer.) Avoid working in the garden after rain. Diseases can spread rap-
idly among wet pepper plants. Weed the garden. Weeds provide a refuge for garden pests and can spread fungi and viruses to nearby healthy pepper plants. agriculture,
